d: Chronic elevation of arterial blood pressure, a major risk factor for stroke, heart failure, and kidney disease.
Metabolic disorder causing chronic hyperglycemia from defects in insulin secretion or action, encompassing type 1, type 2, and gestational forms.
Proactive health services (vaccinations, screenings, counseling) aimed at preventing disease before it develops.
The co-occurrence of two or more distinct medical conditions in the same individual, either related or unrelated to the primary diagnosis.
Ongoing, coordinated care strategies to control long-lasting conditions and maintain quality of life.
Excess body fat above the healthy range, linked to increased risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and certain cancers.
Acute viral inflammation of the upper airways including the nose, pharynx, and larynx.
Patient's voluntary agreement to a proposed medical intervention after receiving adequate information.
The primary symptom or concern that brings a patient to seek medical care, recorded at the start of every encounter.
A comprehensive record of a patient's past illnesses, surgeries, medications, allergies, and family health background.
Clinical process of distinguishing similar conditions to determine the most likely diagnosis based on signs, symptoms, and test findings.
A forecast or prediction of the probable course and eventual outcome of a disease, including likelihood of recovery or progression.
Abnormal immune response to a harmless substance (allergen), producing symptoms from mild irritation to life-threatening anaphylaxis.
A written or electronic instruction from a licensed clinician specifying a medication, dose, route, and duration.
Subjective evidence of disease experienced and reported by the patient (e.g. pain, fatigue, nausea).
Objective evidence of disease detected by a clinician during examination (e.g. fever, swelling, abnormal heart sound).
Five core measurements assessed at each encounter: blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, body temperature, and oxygen saturation.
Sudden-onset, short-duration condition of often intense severity, contrasted with chronic.
Death of myocardial tissue from acute coronary artery occlusion, presenting with chest pain, ECG changes, and elevated cardiac biomarkers.
Spectrum of conditions from acute coronary flow reduction, including unstable angina, NSTEMI, and STEMI, all requiring urgent evaluation.
Paroxysmal chest pain or discomfort caused by transient myocardial ischemia, typically triggered by exertion or stress.
Syndrome in which the heart cannot pump sufficient blood to meet metabolic demands, causing dyspnea, fluid accumulation, and fatigue.
Any abnormality in cardiac rate or rhythm — too fast, too slow, or irregular — ranging from harmless to life-threatening.
Supraventricular arrhythmia with chaotic atrial electrical activity causing irregular heartbeat and increased risk of stroke and embolism.
Diagnostic test recording electrical activity of the heart to detect arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, and conduction abnormalities.
Narrowing or blockage of the coronary arteries due to atherosclerosis, leading to reduced blood supply to the heart.
Lipid plaque buildup in arterial walls causing progressive vessel narrowing, the primary cause of coronary artery disease and stroke.
Sudden loss of neurological function from interruption of blood supply to the brain, classified as ischemic or hemorrhagic.
Ischemic stroke caused by blockage of a cerebral artery, the most common type of stroke.
Stroke from blood vessel rupture within or around the brain, causing intracerebral or subarachnoid bleeding.
Insufficient blood supply to a tissue or organ, causing oxygen deprivation and risk of cell death.
Brief neurological dysfunction from temporary cerebral ischemia that resolves quickly without permanent infarction, serving as a warning sign of impending stroke.
Sudden cessation of effective heart pumping, causing loss of consciousness and death within minutes without CPR.
Escape of blood from ruptured vessels, internally or externally, classified by source (arterial, venous, or capillary) and location.
Subjective awareness of an abnormally fast, slow, or irregular heartbeat, commonly from arrhythmia or anxiety.
The process of identifying a disease or condition based on symptoms, signs, history, and test results.
Any intervention aimed at curing, managing, or alleviating a disease or its symptoms.
Describing a condition persisting over a long period, typically requiring ongoing monitoring and management, contrasted with acute.
Immune response to tissue injury or infection with vasodilation and leukocyte infiltration, manifesting as redness, swelling, heat, and pain.
Invasion and replication of pathogens — bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites — within a host, causing tissue damage and clinical disease.
Body temperature elevated above the normal range, typically indicating infection or systemic inflammation.
Unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage, varying in type, duration, and intensity.
Removal of a tissue sample for microscopic examination to establish or confirm a diagnosis, particularly for suspected malignancy.
A neoplasm or condition that is non-malignant, does not invade adjacent tissue or metastasize, and carries a favorable prognosis.
Describing a tumour that invades surrounding tissue and can metastasize to other parts of the body.
Spread of cancer cells from the primary site to distant organs or tissues via blood or lymphatic system.
A break or crack in a bone caused by trauma, stress, or underlying bone disease such as osteoporosis.
Fluid accumulation in interstitial tissues causing swelling, due to elevated capillary pressure, low albumin, or lymphatic obstruction.
An unpleasant urge to vomit arising from the upper abdomen, occurring as a nonspecific symptom of gastrointestinal and systemic conditions.
Subjective sensation of difficulty or discomfort in breathing, a cardinal symptom of cardiac and pulmonary disease.
Rapid assessment and prioritization of patients by severity to allocate limited medical resources to those who will benefit most.
Progressive decline in memory, language, and reasoning severe enough to impair daily life, most commonly caused by Alzheimer's disease.
Progressive neurodegenerative disease causing dementia via amyloid plaques and tau tangles, resulting in irreversible cognitive decline.
Chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures due to abnormal electrical brain activity.
Sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain causing transient changes in behavior, movement, sensation, or consciousness.
Progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia due to loss of dopamine-producing neurons.
Autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the myelin sheath of nerve fibers, disrupting brain-body communication.
Pain in any head region, classified as primary (migraine, tension-type) or secondary (infection, hypertension, or intracranial pathology).
Recurrent moderate-to-severe headache, often unilateral and throbbing, frequently accompanied by nausea and light sensitivity.
Damage or dysfunction of peripheral nerves causing numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain, commonly due to diabetes.
Decline in memory, language, judgment, or other thinking skills, ranging from mild (MCI) to severe (dementia).
Broad specialty focused on the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of adult diseases not requiring surgery.
Medical specialty dealing with diagnosis and treatment of mental, emotional, and behavioural disorders.
Specialty devoted to the health and medical care of infants, children, and adolescents.
Combined specialty covering pregnancy and childbirth (obstetrics) and female reproductive health (gynecology).
Specialty that uses operative techniques to treat injuries, diseases, and deformities of the body.
Specialty concerned with conditions of the musculoskeletal system including bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and muscles.
Specialty dealing with disorders of the nervous system, including the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.
Medical specialty focused on cardiovascular diseases, encompassing diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of cardiac and vascular disorders.
Specialty dedicated to the study, diagnosis, and treatment of cancer.
Abnormal blood lipid levels — elevated cholesterol, LDL, or triglycerides, or reduced HDL — increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease.
Formal process of directing a patient from a primary care provider to a specialist for further evaluation or treatment.
Concurrent use of multiple medications in one patient, increasing the risk of drug interactions and adverse effects.
Administration of a vaccine to stimulate immunity and protect against specific infectious diseases.
Thyroid hormone deficiency causing metabolic slowdown, manifesting as fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and constipation.
Skeletal disorder with low bone mass and increased fragility raising fracture risk, most prevalent in postmenopausal women and older adults.
Mood disorder with persistent low mood and anhedonia lasting two or more weeks, impairing sleep, appetite, energy, and daily function.
Bacterial infection of the urinary system including the bladder, urethra, or kidneys.
Weight (kg) divided by height squared (m²), used as a screening tool for underweight, overweight, and obesity.
Outpatient medical care provided without hospital admission, where the patient is seen and returns home the same day.
Scheduled subsequent visit to monitor a patient's condition, treatment response, or recovery progress.
Testing of asymptomatic individuals to detect disease or risk factors early, before symptoms develop.
The degree to which a patient correctly follows the prescribed treatment plan, including medications and lifestyle changes.
Persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep despite adequate opportunity, occurring on most nights and causing significant daytime dysfunction.
Lumbar spine pain classified as acute, subacute, or chronic by duration, and a leading cause of musculoskeletal disability worldwide.
Disorders characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry impairing daily life, including generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, and phobias.
Chronic retrograde flow of gastric acid into the esophagus causing heartburn and potentially progressing to esophagitis or Barrett's esophagus.
Reduction in circulating red blood cells or hemoglobin impairing oxygen delivery, most commonly from iron deficiency, chronic disease, or hemolysis.
A non-invasive diagnostic procedure utilizing ultrasound waves to evaluate the real-time structure and function of the heart.
Minimally invasive procedure to open blocked coronary arteries, typically using a balloon and stent.
Percentage of blood ejected from the left ventricle per heartbeat, a key measure of cardiac function used to classify heart failure severity.
A drug that inhibits the coagulation cascade to prevent or treat thrombosis; warfarin (Coumadin), rivaroxaban (Xarelto), and apixaban (Eliquis) are widely used agents.
Implantable electronic device that delivers electrical impulses to regulate abnormally slow or irregular heartbeats.
Heart failure with fluid congestion in the lungs and periphery, presenting as dyspnea, peripheral edema, and reduced exercise tolerance.
Emergency procedure combining chest compressions and rescue breaths to maintain circulation and oxygenation during cardiac arrest.
Imaging using magnetic fields and radio waves to produce cross-sectional images of organs, soft tissues, bones, and neural structures.
X-ray-based imaging that produces cross-sectional anatomical images, serving as the first-line modality for acute trauma, stroke, and thoracic and abdominal assessment.
Needle insertion into the lumbar subarachnoid space to collect cerebrospinal fluid for diagnosis or to administer intrathecal medication.
Acute inflammation of the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord, usually caused by bacterial or viral infection.
An illusory sensation of spinning of oneself or the surroundings caused by vestibular pathology, distinct from nonspecific dizziness.
Nonspecific sensation of lightheadedness or unsteadiness without a spinning quality, distinct from vestibular vertigo.
Group of diseases characterized by progressive loss of nerve cell structure or function, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
Damage to the spinal cord resulting in loss of motor, sensory, or autonomic function below the level of injury.
Recording of the brain's electrical activity via scalp electrodes, used as the primary diagnostic tool for epilepsy and seizure disorders.
Impairment of language ability caused by brain damage, affecting speaking, understanding, reading, or writing.
Involuntary rhythmic muscle contraction causing shaking of one or more body parts, a hallmark feature of Parkinson's disease and essential tremor.
A specific situation or pre-existing clinical condition in which a drug, procedure, or intervention must not be used due to risk of harm.
A drug that relieves pain without causing loss of consciousness; includes acetaminophen (Tylenol), ibuprofen (Advil), and opioids such as morphine (MS Contin).
A medication that reduces elevated body temperature (fever); acetaminophen (Tylenol), ibuprofen (Advil), and aspirin (Bayer) are common agents.
Route of drug or fluid administration directly into a vein, allowing rapid onset and used for urgent treatments, medication delivery, and hydration.
An unintended, harmful response to a drug occurring at normal therapeutic doses, distinct from overdose or medication error.
Action taken or medication administered to prevent the occurrence or development of disease before it manifests.
Drug administration into the fatty tissue just beneath the skin, providing slower absorption than intravenous injection and commonly used for insulin and heparin.
Drug administration directly into muscle tissue, providing faster absorption than subcutaneous injection and used for vaccines and some antibiotics.
Drug delivery by placing medication under the tongue for rapid mucosal absorption, bypassing first-pass hepatic metabolism.
Ability of a treatment to produce the desired therapeutic effect under controlled trial conditions, distinct from effectiveness in real-world practice.
Subspecialty dealing with disorders of the endocrine glands and hormones, including diabetes and thyroid disease.
Specialty focused on conditions affecting the skin, hair, nails, and mucous membranes.
Specialty dealing with the anatomy, physiology, and diseases of the eye.
Specialty dealing with diseases of the ear, nose, throat, and related structures of the head and neck.
Specialty focused on the urinary tract in both sexes and the male reproductive system.
Specialty using medical imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound) to diagnose and guide treatment of disease.
Specialty focused on the immediate diagnosis and treatment of acute illnesses and injuries that require urgent care.
Specialty responsible for administering anesthesia and managing pain before, during, and after surgical and procedural interventions.
Specialty that examines tissues, organs, and bodily fluids to diagnose disease, often via biopsy or autopsy.
Study of how drugs interact with biological systems, encompassing their mechanisms, therapeutic uses, and adverse effects.
Subspecialty focused on the diagnosis and treatment of infections caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites.
Healthcare profession focused on the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of diseases of the teeth, gums, and mouth.
Specialty aimed at restoring function and quality of life in patients with physical impairments due to injury, illness, or disability.
A drug class blocking beta-adrenergic receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure; metoprolol (Lopressor), atenolol (Tenormin), and propranolol (Inderal) are common agents.
Blood tests (e.g. troponin, CK-MB) measuring proteins released by damaged heart muscle, used to diagnose MI.
The degree to which individuals can obtain, process, and understand health information to make informed healthcare decisions.
Legal document stating a patient's wishes regarding medical treatment in the event they become unable to communicate.
Consistent, coordinated care delivery to a patient over time and across care settings, including the ongoing patient-provider relationship.
Specialized care focused on relieving symptoms and improving quality of life for patients with serious illness, independent of cure intent.
Surgical specialty focused on reconstructing or altering the human body for aesthetic or functional restoration purposes.
Surgical specialty addressing diseases of thoracic organs including the lungs, esophagus, trachea, mediastinum, and great vessels.
Surgical specialty for treating conditions of the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nervous system.
Subspecialty focused on autoimmune and inflammatory diseases of the joints, muscles, and connective tissue.
Subspecialty focused on diseases of the respiratory tract and lungs.
Subspecialty focused on kidney diseases, electrolyte disorders, hypertension, and renal replacement therapy.
Subspecialty focused on the digestive system including the stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and gallbladder.
Subspecialty dealing with diseases of the blood and blood-forming organs, including anemia, clotting disorders, and leukemia.
Specialty using radioactive substances to visualise and treat diseases, including PET and bone scans.
A temporary loss of consciousness and muscle tone caused by a transient drop in blood flow to the brain.
Sharp, stabbing chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration, coughing, or thoracic movement, indicating pleural inflammation or irritation.
Vomiting of blood originating from the upper gastrointestinal tract, appearing bright red or as coffee-ground material depending on the rate of bleeding.
The coughing up of blood or blood-stained sputum originating from the bronchi, larynx, trachea, or lungs.
The passage of dark, black, tarry stools containing altered blood, indicative of upper gastrointestinal bleeding.
The passage of fresh, bright red blood per rectum, usually indicating acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
An unpleasant skin sensation provoking the urge to scratch, occurring as a localized or generalized symptom of dermatological or systemic disease.
A bluish discoloration of the skin and mucous membranes resulting from inadequate oxygenation of the blood.
A hollow, muscular tube connecting the oral and nasal cavities to the esophagus and larynx, serving as a common pathway for food and air.
The cartilaginous organ located at the top of the trachea that houses the vocal cords and controls airflow during breathing and phonation.
A large membranous tube reinforced by rings of cartilage, extending from the larynx to the bronchi, conducting air to and from the lungs.
The muscular tube that propels swallowed food and liquids from the pharynx down to the stomach via peristalsis.
The first part of the small intestine immediately beyond the stomach, where chyme mixes with bile and pancreatic enzymes.
The highly vascularized middle segment of the small intestine specialized primarily for nutrient absorption.
The final segment of the small intestine, responsible for absorbing vitamin B12 and bile salts before connecting to the large intestine.
The master endocrine gland at the base of the brain that regulates growth, reproduction, and hormone secretion from other endocrine glands.
A primary lymphoid organ in the superior mediastinum where bone marrow-derived T-lymphocytes differentiate and mature.
A small, pear-shaped organ underneath the liver that stores and concentrates bile before releasing it into the duodenum during digestion.
Non-cancerous prostate enlargement common in aging men, causing lower urinary tract symptoms such as weak stream, urgency, and frequency.
Abnormal accumulation of air in the pleural cavity causing lung collapse, classified as spontaneous, traumatic, or iatrogenic.
The presence or formation of calculi within the gallbladder cavity, which may cause biliary colic or progress to cholecystitis.
A progressive, poorly reversible lung disease characterized by chronic airflow limitation, typically driven by smoking.
A chronic systemic autoimmune disorder characterized by symmetric inflammatory arthritis, primarily targeting synovial joints.
Procedure using a flexible endoscope to directly visualize and treat internal structures such as the gastrointestinal tract or airways.
A minimally invasive surgical technique performed in the abdomen or pelvis through small incisions utilizing a camera.
The placement of a flexible plastic tube into the trachea past the vocal cords to maintain an open airway and assist ventilation.
A clinical procedure where a needle or catheter is inserted into the peritoneal cavity to sample or drain ascitic fluid.
Clinical practice of listening to internal body sounds with a stethoscope to assess heart tones, breath sounds, and bowel activity.
Physical exam technique using manual pressure to assess the size, consistency, tenderness, and position of body structures.
A glycoprotein secreted by the prostate gland whose blood levels serve as a clinical marker for benign prostatic hyperplasia and prostate cancer screening.
Acute or chronic inflammation of the gallbladder wall, typically secondary to cystic duct obstruction by gallstones.
Acute inflammation of the vermiform appendix presenting with right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and fever, treated surgically or with antibiotics.
Inflammation of the pancreas, characterized by premature activation of pancreatic enzymes leading to autodigestion of the organ tissue.
Inflammation of the peritoneum, typically from bacterial contamination after visceral perforation, presenting with diffuse abdominal pain, rigidity, and fever.
The formation of crystalline calculi within the renal pelvis or calyces, causing severe flank pain, hematuria, and urinary obstruction.
Gradual, progressive, and irreversible loss of renal function persisting over the long term.
Total, permanent failure of the kidneys, requiring long-term renal replacement therapy (dialysis or kidney transplantation).
Extracorporeal procedure using a dialyzer to filter toxins, waste, and excess fluid from blood in patients with kidney failure.
Dialysis method using the patient's peritoneum as a semipermeable membrane to remove waste products and excess fluid from the blood.
Distension and dilation of the renal pelvis caused by obstruction of urine outflow, leading to potential kidney damage if untreated.
A disorder of the inner ear characterized by brief, recurrent episodes of spinning vertigo triggered by specific changes in head position.
Bacterial or viral inflammation of the middle ear presenting with ear pain, fever, and fluid behind the tympanic membrane.
Perception of noise or ringing in the ears when no external sound source is present, occurring as a symptom of cochlear, auditory nerve, or systemic conditions.
Bleeding originating from the interior of the nasal cavity, most commonly from the Kiesselbach plexus on the anterior nasal septum.
Inflammation of the paranasal sinuses, most commonly triggered by viral upper respiratory infection, with bacterial superinfection occurring in a minority of cases.
A visual disorder where the eyes point in different directions and fail to remain aligned simultaneously under normal viewing conditions.
Progressive opacification or clouding of the natural crystalline lens of the eye, obstructing the transmission of light onto the retina.
Group of eye diseases with progressive optic nerve damage and visual field loss, most often associated with elevated intraocular pressure.
An involuntary, rapid, and rhythmic oscillation of the eyeballs, which may be horizontal, vertical, or rotatory.
Deterioration of the macula causing central vision loss with preserved peripheral vision, a leading cause of blindness in older adults.
Redness of skin or mucous membranes from increased blood flow in superficial capillaries, associated with inflammation or infection.
Transient raised, pruritic wheals caused by histamine release from dermal mast cells, triggered by allergens, medications, or infections.
An acute, spreading bacterial infection of the deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue, presenting with redness, warmth, swelling, and pain.
A chronic, pruritic, inflammatory skin disease that typically begins in infancy or childhood and follows a relapsing course.
Chronic immune-mediated skin disorder causing well-demarcated, scaly erythematous plaques, most commonly on the scalp, elbows, and knees.
An abnormal dermal sensation (such as tingling, pricking, or numbness) experienced without any clear external sensory stimulus.
Severe radiating pain originating in the lower back and traveling down the path of the sciatic nerve through the buttock and posterior leg.
Inflammatory arthritis caused by monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints, classically presenting with sudden, severe pain in the first metatarsophalangeal joint.
Excessive outward curvature of the thoracic spine producing a rounded or hunched posture.
Excess thyroid hormone production causing a hypermetabolic state with weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance, and tremor.
A metabolic disorder caused by prolonged systemic exposure to abnormally elevated levels of glucocorticoids (cortisol).
Rare water metabolism disorder from ADH deficiency or renal resistance, causing excessive urine output, intense thirst, and dehydration.
Progressive microvascular complications of long-standing diabetes mellitus affecting the blood vessels of the retina.
An abnormal reduction in the number of platelets in peripheral blood, increasing bleeding risk and causing petechiae or easy bruising.
An increase in the number of circulating white blood cells above normal range, typically a response to infection or severe inflammation.
Myeloproliferative disorder with uncontrolled red blood cell overproduction, increasing blood viscosity and risk of thrombosis and stroke.
Thrombus formation in the deep veins, typically of the lower limbs, with risk of embolization to the pulmonary circulation causing pulmonary embolism.
Sudden blockage of a pulmonary artery by a blood clot, usually from deep vein thrombosis, causing acute respiratory distress.
Aortic wall tear that allows blood to split between layers creating a false lumen, constituting a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency.
Surgical removal of the gallbladder, most commonly by laparoscopy, serving as the primary treatment for symptomatic cholelithiasis and acute cholecystitis.
Surgical removal of the vermiform appendix, the standard treatment for acute appendicitis, performed by open or laparoscopic technique.
Insertion of a needle or catheter through the chest wall into the pleural space to drain fluid or air for diagnosis or therapeutic relief.
Surgical removal of the uterus for fibroids, endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, or gynecologic malignancy, performed as a partial or total procedure.
A specialized, low-dose X-ray imaging procedure used to visualize internal breast tissue structures for early detection of cancer.
An endoscopic examination evaluating the inner lining of the large intestine and rectum via a flexible video scope.
A fatty deposit or plaque that forms on the inner lining of an arterial wall, characteristic of atherosclerosis.
Abnormal enlargement of the heart, usually resulting from chronic hypertension or heart failure.
A cardiac arrhythmia characterized by a slow heart rate, typically fewer than 60 beats per minute in adults.
A cardiac arrhythmia characterized by a rapid heart rate, typically exceeding 100 beats per minute in adults at rest.
A sudden, significant drop in blood pressure that occurs when moving to an upright position.
Inflammation of the pericardium often presenting with chest pain that worsens with breathing and improves when leaning forward.
An infection of the endocardium, most commonly involving the heart valves, typically caused by bacteria entering the bloodstream.
Cramping pain and weakness in the legs induced by exercise and relieved by rest, caused by peripheral artery disease.
A localized, abnormal dilation or ballooning of a blood vessel wall, carrying a permanent risk of rupture.
Lung tissue collapse or incomplete expansion impairing gas exchange, occurring postoperatively or from mucus plugging and external compression.
A chronic, progressive respiratory disease characterized by permanent dilation and destruction of the bronchial walls.
An abnormal accumulation of fluid in the pleural cavity surrounding the lungs.
A collection of whole blood in the pleural cavity, usually resulting from trauma or chest surgery.
Accumulation of pus within a body cavity, most commonly the pleural space, typically resulting from pneumonia, thoracic surgery, or chest trauma.
Abnormal elevation of arterial carbon dioxide, typically from hypoventilation or respiratory failure, leading to respiratory acidosis.
Abnormally low oxygen levels in arterial blood, indicating impaired respiratory or circulatory function.
A harsh, high-pitched, vibrating respiratory sound caused by turbulent airflow through a partially obstructed upper airway.
Chronic progressive scarring of the lung interstitium impairing gas exchange, with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) being the most common form.
Abnormal fluid accumulation in the peritoneal cavity, most commonly caused by portal hypertension from cirrhosis, malignancy, or heart failure.
Irreversible hepatic scarring from chronic liver injury that impairs function, with complications including portal hypertension and liver failure.
Difficulty swallowing, classified as oropharyngeal (mouth-to-esophagus transfer) or esophageal (transit through the esophagus) in origin.
Inflammation of colonic diverticula, most commonly in the sigmoid colon, presenting with left lower quadrant pain, fever, and leukocytosis.
A yellowish discoloration of the skin, sclera, and mucous membranes caused by hyperbilirubinemia.
A serious condition in which part of the intestine slides or telescopes into an adjacent part, obstructing blood supply.
A loop of intestine twisting around itself and the mesentery that supports it, resulting in mechanical bowel obstruction.
Any bleeding originating from the digestive tract, classified broadly into upper (UGIB) and lower (LGIB).
A chronic syndrome characterized by delayed gastric emptying of solids or liquids without mechanical obstruction.
An abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) within the ventricles of the brain, increasing intracranial pressure.
Lack of voluntary coordination of muscle movements, frequently resulting from cerebellar dysfunction or nerve damage.
Intense, typically burning or stabbing pain radiating along the course of one or more specific nerves.
Motor speech disorder from neurological injury causing slurred, slow, or imprecise pronunciation despite intact language comprehension.
Brain dysfunction presenting with confusion or altered consciousness, arising from metabolic, toxic, infectious, or structural causes.
Acute inflammation of the brain parenchyma, usually viral, presenting with fever, headache, altered consciousness, and focal neurological deficits.
The presence of abnormal amounts of protein in the urine, often an early clinical indicator of renal disease.
The abnormal presence of red blood cells in the urine, categorized into gross (visible) and microscopic.
The production of an abnormally small volume of urine, often indicating kidney dysfunction or reduced renal perfusion.
Cessation or near-complete absence of urine production indicating severe acute or chronic kidney failure.
Immunologically mediated inflammation of the glomeruli, the microscopic filtering units of the kidney.
A collection of clinical findings caused by kidney damage, including massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and severe edema.
Bacterial infection of the renal parenchyma, typically from ascending urinary tract infection, presenting with fever, flank pain, and costovertebral angle tenderness.
Inflammation of the urinary bladder wall, most frequently caused by an ascending bacterial infection.
Involuntary loss of urine classified as stress (exertion-triggered), urge (overactive bladder), overflow, or mixed type.
Pain, burning, or discomfort experienced during the process of urination, typical of lower urinary tract infections.
Elevation of blood glucose above normal levels, the hallmark of diabetes mellitus and a risk factor for acute and chronic diabetic complications.
Abnormally low blood glucose causing autonomic symptoms such as sweating and tremor, and neuroglycopenic effects such as confusion.
Life-threatening complication of diabetes characterized by severe hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and elevated ketones, most common in type 1 diabetes.
An abnormal benign proliferation of glandular breast tissue in males, caused by an imbalance between estrogen and androgen action.
Chronic disorder from a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma after epiphyseal closure, causing progressive enlargement of hands, feet, and face.
The spontaneous, inappropriate secretion of breast milk unrelated to childbearing or nursing, often linked to hyperprolactinemia.

en_l: Excessive male-pattern hair growth in women
d: Excessive growth of thick, dark hair in females following a male-like distribution pattern, resulting from androgen excess.
Overactivity of the parathyroid glands resulting in excess production of parathyroid hormone (PTH), causing hypercalcemia.

en_l: Adrenal tumor causing high blood pressure spike
d: A rare, neuroendocrine tumor of the adrenal medulla or extra-adrenal chromaffin tissue that secretes catecholamines.
Cluster of metabolic risk factors — abdominal obesity, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and hyperglycemia — that elevates cardiovascular risk.

en_l: Red, white, and platelet cell drop
d: Simultaneous reduction in red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, caused by bone marrow failure, nutritional deficiency, or malignancy.
An abnormally low concentration of neutrophils (a type of white blood cell) in the blood, severely raising bacterial infection risks.
Enlargement of one or more lymph nodes, occurring as a reactive response to localized or systemic infection, inflammation, or malignancy.
Pathological enlargement of the spleen, commonly resulting from portal hypertension, hematologic malignancies, or chronic infections.
Systemic coagulopathy from an underlying trigger that consumes clotting factors, causing simultaneous widespread thrombosis and hemorrhage.
Malignant neoplasm of epithelial origin, the most common cancer category, encompassing tumors of the lung, breast, colon, prostate, and other organs.
A malignant tumor arising from mesenchymal supportive or connective tissues, such as bone, cartilage, fat, or muscle.

en_l: Target body's immune system to fight cancer
d: A type of cancer treatment that stimulates or restores the body's natural immune system mechanisms to detect and fight malignant cells.
Additional cancer treatment given after the primary treatment (such as surgery) to lower the risk of recurrence.
Treatment (chemotherapy, radiation, or targeted therapy) given before surgery to reduce tumor size and improve resectability.
Absence of menstruation, classified as primary (menarche never occurred) or secondary (cessation of previously established cycles).
Painful menstruation or severe uterine cramping during menstrual bleeding, causing functional impairment.
A benign non-cancerous tumor of the uterine smooth muscle tissue, extremely common in women of reproductive age.
The premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall prior to the second stage of labor.
Excessive blood loss following childbirth, after either vaginal delivery or cesarean section, constituting an obstetric emergency.
A rapid, severe, and potentially life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction causing airway occlusion and vascular collapse.
Describing a disease or condition that arises spontaneously with no identifiable or known cause.
An adverse condition, illness, or injury induced inadvertently by a physician, medical team, drug therapy, or diagnostic procedure.
Increase in organ or tissue size from enlargement of individual cells, commonly seen in cardiac muscle responding to sustained increased workload.
Increase in cell number within a tissue or organ causing enlargement, occurring as a physiological response or pathological process such as prostatic hyperplasia.
Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection, representing a medical emergency requiring prompt treatment.

en_l: Inability to recognize objects or faces
d: The inability to process sensory information and recognize objects or persons despite intact sensory organs.

en_l: Lack of awareness of one's own illness
d: Neurological condition in which a patient is unaware of or denies their own disability or illness, commonly seen after stroke or in dementia.
A behavioral syndrome characterized by abnormal movements, immobility, mutism, or extreme unresponsiveness.
A movement disorder characterized by a subjective feeling of inner restlessness and a compelling need to move.
A neurological disorder featuring involuntary, repetitive facial movements caused by long-term neuroleptic drug use.
Abnormal and often painful sensitivity to light, commonly associated with migraine, meningitis, uveitis, and corneal injury.
The gradual, progressive, and bilateral loss of hearing that occurs naturally with aging.
Perception of two images of a single object, most commonly caused by extraocular muscle weakness or cranial nerve palsy.
Degenerative joint disease from progressive cartilage breakdown, the most common form of arthritis, causing pain, stiffness, and reduced joint mobility.

en_l: Chronic spinal arthritis that fuses bones
d: A chronic, progressive inflammatory arthritis affecting the sacroiliac joints and axial skeleton.
The degenerative loss of skeletal muscle mass, quality, and strength associated with aging.
Pain in one or more muscles, occurring as localized (trauma, overuse) or diffuse (viral illness, fibromyalgia, statin myopathy) presentations.
Abnormal lateral curvature of the spine, typically presenting in adolescence and classified as idiopathic, congenital, or neuromuscular in origin.

en_l: Bone tissue death from lack of blood supply
d: The cellular death of bone components due to temporary or permanent interruption of blood supply.
Inflammation of a tendon and its protective fluid-filled sheath from repetitive strain.
The partial or complete absence or loss of hair from areas where it normally grows.

en_l: Tiny purple or red skin spots
d: Pinpoint, round, non-raised purplish-red spots caused by minor intradermal hemorrhage.

en_l: Purple skin blotches from internal bleeding
d: Non-blanching purple skin discoloration from subdermal bleeding, indicating platelet disorders, vasculitis, or coagulopathy.
A larger area of subcutaneous bleeding resulting from vascular leakage, commonly known as a bruise.
A rapidly progressive, life-threatening destructive infection of subcutaneous tissue and fascia.
A gynecological emergency involving rotation of the ovary, compromising arterial inflow.

en_l: Fainting from sudden blood pressure drop
d: A transient loss of consciousness mediated by an inappropriate reflex causing bradycardia and vasodilation.
Atherosclerotic narrowing of peripheral arteries reducing limb blood flow, classically presenting as intermittent claudication during physical activity.

en_l: White fingers from cold or stress
d: Episodic digital vasospasm from cold or stress causing sequential pallor, cyanosis, and erythema in the fingers or toes.
Chronic swelling of tissues caused by an accumulation of lymphatic fluid due to obstruction.
A life-threatening compression of the heart due to rapid accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac.

en_l: Stones in the main bile duct
d: Presence of gallstones in the common bile duct, potentially causing biliary obstruction, jaundice, cholangitis, or pancreatitis.
Bacterial infection of the bile ducts from biliary obstruction, classically presenting with fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain.
Dilated submucosal veins in the esophagus caused by portal hypertension, with rupture causing potentially fatal upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

en_l: Stomach lining changing to look like bowel
d: The transformation of gastric mucosa into a cellular phenotype resembling intestinal mucosa.
Failure of intestinal peristalsis causing functional bowel obstruction, presenting with abdominal distension, nausea, vomiting, and absent bowel sounds.
Dyspnea that occurs when lying flat, forcing the individual to remain upright to breathe.
Severe inflammatory lung injury causing diffuse alveolar damage and hypoxemia, most often triggered by sepsis, pneumonia, or major trauma.

en_l: Percentage of drug absorbed into blood
d: The fraction of an administered drug dose that reaches the systemic circulation unchanged.
An early, non-specific symptom indicating the onset of a disease before major signs appear.

en_l: Conditions left behind after a disease
d: A pathological condition resulting from a prior, acute disease or injury.
Progressive weight loss, muscle wasting, and fatigue associated with advanced cancer, heart failure, or chronic infection.

en_l: Dangerously low blood pressure from blood infection
d: Subset of sepsis with circulatory failure, defined by persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors and elevated lactate despite adequate fluid resuscitation.
Elevated serum potassium that can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation.
Surgical creation of an opening through the anterior neck into the trachea, indicated for prolonged mechanical ventilation or upper airway obstruction.
Dome-shaped musculotendinous partition separating the thoracic and abdominal cavities, serving as the principal muscle of inhalation.
One of two large airways branching from the trachea into each lung, further dividing into bronchioles to distribute air through the lungs.
Microscopic air sac at the end of a bronchiole where oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange between air and blood across thin capillary walls.
Double-layered serous membrane lining the thoracic cavity and covering each lung, with the intervening pleural space allowing frictionless respiratory movement.
Leaf-shaped cartilage at the base of the tongue that folds over the airway during swallowing to prevent aspiration into the trachea.
The muscular middle layer of the heart wall, composed of cardiomyocytes responsible for the contractile force that pumps blood.
Fibroserous double-walled sac enclosing the heart, with inner serous layers secreting fluid to reduce friction during cardiac contraction.
One of the two upper heart chambers, with the right receiving deoxygenated blood from the body and the left receiving oxygenated blood from the lungs.
Either of the two lower heart chambers that eject blood: the right pumps to the lungs, the left pumps oxygenated blood to the body.
The largest artery in the body, arising from the left ventricle and distributing oxygenated blood throughout the systemic circulation.
One of two large veins returning deoxygenated blood to the right atrium, with the superior vena cava draining the upper body and the inferior vena cava the lower body.
Distal stomach outlet connecting to the duodenum, where the pyloric sphincter regulates the rate of gastric emptying into the small intestine.

en_l: Pouch at start of large intestine
d: Blind-ended pouch at the junction of the ileum and ascending colon, from whose wall the vermiform appendix projects.
A narrow, blind-ended tube attached to the cecum containing dense lymphoid tissue.
Major portion of the large intestine extending from the cecum to the rectum, absorbing water and electrolytes and compacting fecal matter into stool.
Terminal segment of the large intestine connecting the sigmoid colon to the anal canal, serving as the reservoir for formed stool until defecation.
Mixed gland posterior to the stomach whose exocrine portion secretes digestive enzymes and whose endocrine islets secrete insulin and glucagon.
Functional unit of the kidney comprising a glomerulus and tubular system that filters blood and produces urine.
Tuft of capillaries within Bowman's capsule in the renal cortex, serving as the site of plasma ultrafiltration and the first step in urine formation.
A paired muscular tube that transports urine from the renal pelvis to the urinary bladder via peristaltic contractions.
Tube conveying urine from the bladder to the exterior of the body, which in males also serves as the passage for ejaculation.
Largest brain region, divided into two hemispheres and four lobes, governing thought, memory, language, sensation, and voluntary movement.
The region of the brain located at the back of the skull responsible for coordinating voluntary movement, balance, and fine motor control.
Structure connecting the cerebrum to the spinal cord via the medulla, pons, and midbrain, controlling vital functions including breathing and heart rate.
Three protective layers surrounding the brain and spinal cord — dura mater, arachnoid mater, and pia mater — whose inflammation constitutes meningitis.
Medial temporal lobe structure critical for forming new declarative memories and one of the earliest regions affected in Alzheimer's disease.
Cylindrical nerve tissue column in the vertebral canal transmitting motor signals from brain to body and sensory signals from body to brain.
Curved bone connecting the sternum to the scapula and forming the shoulder girdle, the most commonly fractured bone in the upper extremity.
Flat triangular posterior shoulder bone articulating with the clavicle and humerus, providing attachment for major upper limb muscles.
Flat midline thoracic bone comprising the manubrium, body, and xiphoid process, articulating with the clavicles and costal cartilages of the upper ribs.
The longest and strongest bone in the body, extending from the hip to the knee as the sole bone of the thigh.
Triangular sesamoid bone embedded in the quadriceps tendon that articulates with the femur, protecting the knee joint and improving quadriceps leverage.
Fibrocartilaginous disc between vertebral bodies consisting of an annulus fibrosus and nucleus pulposus, absorbing spinal loads and enabling flexible movement.
Band of dense fibrous tissue connecting bone to bone across a joint, providing stability and limiting excessive movement.
Dense collagen cord connecting skeletal muscle to bone, transmitting the force of muscular contraction to produce joint movement.
The outermost, avascular skin layer providing a protective barrier against pathogens, UV radiation, and water loss.
Dense connective tissue layer beneath the epidermis containing collagen, elastin, blood vessels, nerve endings, hair follicles, and glands.
Transparent dome-shaped outermost layer of the eye that refracts incoming light and protects the iris and pupil from foreign material.
Light-sensitive posterior eye layer containing rods and cones that convert light into neural signals transmitted via the optic nerve.
Spiral inner ear structure with hair cells that convert sound vibrations into electrical nerve impulses transmitted via the auditory nerve.
Small immune organs along lymphatic vessels that filter lymph and concentrate immune cells to detect and respond to infection and malignancy.
Butterfly-shaped endocrine gland in the neck producing T3 and T4 hormones that regulate metabolism, energy, growth, and body temperature.
Hollow, pear-shaped muscular organ in the female pelvis that houses a developing fetus and periodically sheds its lining to produce menstrual flow.
Male gland surrounding the urethra below the bladder that contributes to seminal fluid production, prone to benign enlargement and malignancy with aging.
Specialty focused on the diagnosis and management of life-threatening conditions requiring continuous organ support and monitoring.
Surgical specialty focused on diseases of the arterial, venous, and lymphatic systems, excluding the heart and brain.
Surgical specialty dealing with disorders of the colon, rectum, and anus, including cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and hemorrhoids.
The branch of medicine studying the immune system, including allergic, autoimmune, and immunodeficiency diseases.
Complete, reversible loss of consciousness induced by anesthetic agents, allowing painless surgery without awareness.
Reversible loss of sensation in a specific body area by blocking nerve conduction, without affecting consciousness.
Administration of medication to reduce anxiety, induce calm, or produce a sleep-like state, ranging from minimal to deep sedation.
Injection of local anesthetic into the epidural space to block lower-body pain, used in labor analgesia and postoperative pain management.
Funnel-shaped cavity at the center of each kidney collecting urine from the calyces and channeling it into the ureter.
Injection of local anesthetic around a specific nerve or nerve bundle to block sensation in a targeted area without affecting consciousness.
A drug-induced state of reduced consciousness allowing the patient to respond to verbal commands, used for minor procedures.
Local anesthetic injection into the lumbar subarachnoid space producing temporary loss of sensation and movement below the injection level.
Imaging technique using high-frequency sound waves to visualize internal organs and structures in real time without ionizing radiation.
Imaging modality using ionizing radiation to visualize bone and lung pathology, the most widely used diagnostic imaging technique worldwide.
A substance administered before imaging to enhance the visibility of blood vessels, organs, or lesions on CT, MRI, or X-ray.
Treatment using exercise, manual therapy, and physical modalities to restore function and mobility and reduce pain after injury or illness.
Therapy focused on helping patients regain the ability to perform daily activities after injury, illness, or disability.
The extent of movement achievable at a joint, measured to assess injury, monitor recovery, and guide rehabilitation.
Therapeutic intervention for communication, speech, language, voice, and swallowing disorders from neurological or developmental conditions.
Use of cytotoxic drugs to kill or suppress cancer cell growth, administered orally or intravenously and commonly causing systemic side effects.
Hollow muscular organ that circulates blood via rhythmic contractions, consisting of four chambers: two atria and two ventricles.
Blood vessels supplying oxygenated blood directly to the heart muscle, whose blockage causes angina or myocardial infarction.
One of four structures (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary) that ensure unidirectional blood flow through the heart chambers.
Smallest blood vessels connecting arterioles to venules, serving as the site of gas, nutrient, and waste exchange between blood and tissues.
Paired spongy organs in the thorax performing gas exchange — absorbing oxygen from inhaled air and expelling carbon dioxide per breath.
The hollow space inside the nose that warms, humidifies, and filters inhaled air before it reaches the lower respiratory tract.
Air-filled cavities within the skull bones surrounding the nasal cavity, which become inflamed in sinusitis.
A muscular, J-shaped organ between the esophagus and small intestine that stores food, secretes gastric acid, and begins protein digestion.
The largest internal organ, located in the upper right abdomen, performing metabolism, detoxification, protein synthesis, and bile production.
Coiled tube (~6-7 m) between the stomach and large intestine — duodenum, jejunum, and ileum — and the primary site of nutrient absorption.
Final section of the digestive tract consisting of the cecum, colon, and rectum, absorbing water and electrolytes and forming stool.
The S-shaped final segment of the colon connecting to the rectum, a common site for diverticulosis and colorectal cancer.
A pair of bean-shaped organs in the retroperitoneum that filter blood, regulate fluid and electrolyte balance, and produce urine.
A hollow, muscular organ in the pelvis that stores urine produced by the kidneys until it is expelled through the urethra.
Central organ of the nervous system enclosed within the skull, governing thought, memory, sensation, movement, and autonomic bodily functions.
Bundle of axons in connective sheaths that transmit electrical signals between the CNS and target organs, muscles, and sensory receptors.
Fundamental nervous system cell that receives, integrates, and transmits information via electrical impulses and chemical synaptic signals.
Rigid connective tissue forming the skeleton that supports the body, protects organs, enables movement, and produces blood cells in the marrow.
Contractile tissue classified as skeletal (voluntary), cardiac, or smooth (involuntary), producing movement, maintaining posture, and driving organ function.
Firm, flexible avascular connective tissue covering joint surfaces and forming the nose, ears, and intervertebral cushions, with limited capacity for self-repair.
The long bone of the upper arm extending from the shoulder joint to the elbow, commonly fractured in falls on an outstretched hand.
The larger of the two lower leg bones, bearing most of the body weight and forming the medial aspect of the ankle joint.
One of the bones forming the vertebral column, protecting the spinal cord and providing structural support for the trunk.
Paired endocrine gland above each kidney whose cortex secretes cortisol and aldosterone and whose medulla secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine during stress.
A fist-sized lymphoid organ in the upper left abdomen that filters blood, recycles red blood cells, and plays a role in immune response.
A pair of female reproductive glands that produce eggs (ova) and secrete the hormones estrogen and progesterone.
The lower, narrow portion of the uterus connecting to the vagina, routinely screened for cancer via cervical cytology.
A pair of male reproductive glands in the scrotum that produce sperm and testosterone.
The pigmented, contractile circular membrane of the eye that controls pupil size, regulating the amount of light reaching the retina.
Transparent biconvex structure behind the iris that focuses light onto the retina, becoming opaque in cataract.
Thin membrane separating the outer ear canal from the middle ear that vibrates in response to sound waves and transmits them to the ossicles.
The region between the thorax and pelvis, containing the stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas, and kidneys.
Movement of a limb or body part away from the midline of the body, or in the digits, away from the central finger or toe.
Movement of a limb or body part toward the midline of the body, or in the digits, toward the central finger or toe.
Malignant tumor arising from glandular epithelial cells, the most common cancer type in the lung, colon, breast, and prostate.
Connective tissue of fat cells serving as energy storage, insulation, and organ cushioning.
Able to use both hands with equal skill, a characteristic noted in neurological and occupational assessments.
Deliberate artificial rupture of the amniotic membranes using a sterile instrument to induce or accelerate labor.
Hormones including testosterone that promote male secondary characteristics, produced by the testes, ovaries, and adrenal glands.
Imaging using contrast dye and X-rays to visualise blood vessels and detect blockages or aneurysms.
Complete absence of oxygen delivery to tissues, more severe than hypoxia and causing rapid cell death if prolonged.
A drug that kills or inhibits bacterial growth; amoxicillin (Amoxil), azithromycin (Zithromax), and ciprofloxacin (Cipro) are commonly prescribed examples.
Hardening and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, a broader category that encompasses atherosclerosis and other vessel-stiffening processes.
Inflammation of the brachial artery, associated with systemic vasculitis or local trauma.
Inflammation of one or more joints causing pain, swelling, and stiffness, encompassing a broad range of conditions including osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis.
Needle aspiration of synovial fluid from a joint for diagnostic analysis or therapeutic decompression.
Fiber-optic instrument inserted through a small incision into a joint to visualize structures for diagnosis and minimally invasive surgery.
A minimally invasive procedure using an arthroscope to diagnose and treat joint problems.
The major vein draining the arm and shoulder, commonly accessed for central venous catheter placement.
An agent that kills bacteria, including antibiotics, disinfectants, and antiseptics.
A two-headed muscle on the front of the upper arm responsible for elbow flexion and forearm supination.
Involuntary forceful contraction of the eyelid muscles, occurring as an isolated benign condition or a sign of neurological disease.
Abnormally short, broad skull shape resulting from premature coronal suture fusion or positional deformity, associated with certain genetic syndromes.
Abnormally slow breathing rate, indicating central nervous system depression, opioid overdose, or other causes of respiratory suppression.
Inflammation of the bronchial tubes, classified as acute (usually viral and self-limiting) or chronic (persistent mucus-producing cough recurring over successive years).
The principal cheek muscle that compresses the cheeks during chewing and contributes to speech and swallowing.
Originating from or caused by the heart, as in cardiogenic shock, which is life-threatening circulatory failure from cardiac pump dysfunction.
Relating to the eight small wrist bones connecting the forearm to the hand.
Narrow passageway at the wrist through which the median nerve and flexor tendons pass, whose compression causes numbness, tingling, and hand weakness.
Pain in any part of the head, classified as primary (migraine, tension-type) or secondary (due to infection, hypertension, or intracranial pathology).
Describing direction or developmental progression from head toward feet, applied to growth patterns and neurological maturation.
Relating to the anatomical angle between the cerebellum and pons, a common site for vestibular schwannomas and other posterior fossa tumors.
Clear fluid circulating around the brain and spinal cord that provides cushioning and waste removal, sampled via lumbar puncture for diagnostic purposes.
Immature cell responsible for producing cartilage matrix, playing an important role in skeletal development and cartilage repair.
Thread-like structures of DNA and protein within cell nuclei that carry the genetic information governing cell function and heredity.
Surgical stoma connecting a portion of the colon to the abdominal surface to divert fecal output into an external collection pouch.
Abnormal connection between the colon and bladder, most commonly from diverticular disease or colorectal cancer, presenting with fecaluria, pneumaturia, and recurrent UTIs.
Relating to or affecting the opposite side of the body, commonly used in neurological and anatomical descriptions.
Inflammation of costal cartilages at the sternum, causing chest pain mimicking cardiac pain.
Temporary removal of a skull section to access the brain for tumor resection, hematoma evacuation, or aneurysm repair.
A tubular instrument with camera and light to examine the bladder and urethra.
Toxic to living cells, a property exploited by chemotherapy drugs that target rapidly dividing cancer cells.
Fluoroscopic examination of the duodenum using contrast medium to evaluate the mucosa, periampullary region, and surrounding structures.
Having lost all natural teeth, requiring prosthetic management and special airway consideration during anesthesia.
The device recording heart electrical activity to produce the ECG tracing.
Recording of cardiac electrical activity via skin electrodes, used to diagnose arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, and conduction abnormalities.
Smooth membrane lining the inner surfaces of the heart chambers and valves, whose inflammation constitutes endocarditis.
A tubular instrument with camera and light for visualising hollow organs or body cavities.
Simultaneous inflammation of the small intestine and colon, caused by infection, Crohn's disease, or other inflammatory conditions.
Anucleate red blood cell containing hemoglobin that transports oxygen from the lungs to tissues and returns carbon dioxide for exhalation.
Steroid hormones from the ovaries regulating the female reproductive system, bone density, and cardiovascular health.
Forearm muscles that flex the fingers at the interphalangeal joints, assessed in hand and peripheral nerve injury evaluation.
Relating to both frontal and parietal bones or lobes of the skull or brain.
Inflammation of the gastric mucosa caused by H. pylori, NSAIDs, or alcohol, presenting with epigastric pain and nausea.
Herniation or protrusion of the stomach through a structural defect.
Medial angulation of the knees toward the midline, a normal developmental finding in young children but pathological when persistent or pronounced.
Lateral bowing of the legs at the knees, a normal finding in early infancy that becomes pathological when persistent beyond typical developmental resolution.
Glucose intolerance first diagnosed during pregnancy that increases perinatal complication risk and predisposes the mother to type 2 diabetes.
Portable device that measures blood glucose concentration from a small capillary sample, essential for diabetes self-monitoring.
Metabolic pathway that breaks down glucose into pyruvate to produce ATP, serving as the first and universal step in cellular respiration.
Surgical removal of approximately half the stomach for peptic ulcer disease or gastric cancer.
Weakness affecting one side of the body, typically from stroke or brain injury.
Complete paralysis of one side of the body from damage to the motor cortex or brainstem.
The physiological process stopping bleeding through vasoconstriction, platelet aggregation, and coagulation.
Abnormal enlargement of the liver, caused by hepatitis, cirrhosis, congestive heart failure, or hepatic malignancy.
A chronic painful skin condition causing abscesses and scarring in sweat gland areas (hidradenitis suppurativa).
Blockage of intestinal content passage from adhesions, hernias, or tumors, potentially causing bowel ischemia and requiring urgent intervention.
Relating to or affecting the same side of the body, commonly used in neurological and anatomical descriptions.
Relating to both the lips and teeth, as in sounds produced by contact between the upper teeth and lower lip, such as /f/ and /v/.
The almond-shaped gland in the upper outer orbit producing tears to lubricate and protect the eye.
Surgical incision through the abdominal wall to access the peritoneal cavity for exploration, emergency intervention, or planned repair.
Sudden involuntary contraction of the laryngeal muscles causing vocal cord closure and airway obstruction, constituting a medical emergency.
The first day of the most recent menstrual period, used as the reference point for calculating gestational age and estimated due date.
White blood cell classified as granulocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils) or agranulocytes (lymphocytes, monocytes), collectively forming the cellular immune defense.
Relating to both the tongue and gingiva, used in dental anatomy to describe structures or surfaces at their interface.
Procedure to fragment kidney or ureteral stones using focused extracorporeal shock waves or endoscopic laser or ultrasound energy.
Abnormal enlargement of the tongue, associated with Down syndrome, acromegaly, hypothyroidism, and amyloidosis.
Nutritional imbalance that impairs physiological function, encompassing undernutrition, overnutrition, and micronutrient deficiency.
Malignant tumor arising from melanocytes, the most dangerous form of skin cancer due to its high metastatic potential.
One of two C-shaped fibrocartilaginous structures in the knee joint that cushion and stabilize the joint, commonly torn in sports injuries.
The sum of all chemical reactions in living cells that maintain life, comprising catabolic (breakdown) and anabolic (synthesis) processes.
Relating to the five long bones of the hand connecting wrist to finger bones.
Inflammation and ulceration of mucous membranes, a common and painful complication of chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
Relating to the muscles and skeleton as a combined system that provides structure, enables movement, and protects vital organs.
The study of fungi including medically important species causing candidiasis and aspergillosis.
Immature bone marrow precursor cell that develops into granulocytes, with elevated circulating counts characteristic of acute myeloid leukemia.
Imaging using contrast dye injected into the spinal canal to visualise spinal cord and nerve roots.
Malignant proliferation of plasma cells in the bone marrow causing bone destruction, anemia, hypercalcemia, and immune impairment.
Fungal infection of the tympanic membrane, typically associated with prolonged antibiotic use or chronic ear moisture.
The uppermost region of the pharynx lying behind the nasal cavity, containing the adenoids and the openings of the Eustachian tubes.
Relating to the first 28 days of life, a critical period for identifying and managing congenital conditions and birth-related complications.
Surgical removal of a kidney for renal cancer, severe trauma, or living donation.
The formation of new neurons, occurring primarily during fetal development with limited continuation in certain adult brain regions.
Having blood pressure within the normal range.
Relating to eye movement, with cranial nerve III controlling most extraocular movements, pupillary constriction, and eyelid elevation.
A mutated or overexpressed gene that promotes uncontrolled cell division, derived from normal proto-oncogenes by mutation or amplification.
The middle region of the pharynx containing the tonsils, soft palate, and tongue base, and a site for tonsillitis and HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer.
A bony outgrowth at the margins of a joint resulting from cartilage degeneration, causing pain and restricting movement in osteoarthritis.
Discharge from the ear canal, which may be watery (cerebrospinal fluid leak), purulent (infection), or bloody (trauma).
A handheld instrument with light and magnifying lens for examining the ear canal and tympanic membrane.
Complete loss of voluntary muscle function resulting from nervous system damage, which may be temporary or permanent depending on the cause.
Circumferential compression device for unstable pelvic fractures that reduces pelvic volume and controls life-threatening hemorrhage.
Needle aspiration of fluid from the pericardial sac, performed as an emergency procedure to relieve cardiac tamponade.
Serious bacterial infection of the gums and supporting bone caused by plaque accumulation, and a leading cause of tooth loss in adults.
Dense fibrous membrane covering the outer surfaces of bone, containing blood vessels and osteoblasts that supply nutrition and enable bone repair.
Surrounding or adjacent to blood vessels, a term applied to inflammatory infiltrates seen in vasculitis and autoimmune conditions.
A graphic recording of heart sounds and murmurs for cardiac auscultation documentation.
Obliteration of the pleural space to prevent recurrent effusions or pneumothorax.
Abnormally excessive thirst, a classic symptom of diabetes mellitus and diabetes insipidus.
Abnormally large daily urine output, a cardinal symptom of diabetes mellitus, diabetes insipidus, and other conditions impairing urinary concentration.
Located behind the ear, a region used for cochlear implant surgical incisions and lymph node assessment.
Relating to the period immediately following surgery, encompassing pain management, wound care, and clinical monitoring.
Occurring after childbirth, referring to the period following delivery as the mother's body recovers.
Occurring or provided before birth, encompassing the medical care, monitoring, and screening performed during pregnancy.
Relating to the period before surgery, encompassing history taking, physical examination, investigations, and informed consent.
A dental specialist focused on restoring and replacing teeth using dentures, bridges, and implants.
Extension of a structure forward beyond its normal surface, referring in dentistry to jaw position and in spinal medicine to disc herniation.
A nuclear medicine scan using a tracer to assess kidney blood flow, function, and drainage.
Surgical reshaping of the nose for cosmetic or functional purposes.
Excessive nasal discharge that may be clear (viral infection, allergy, or cerebrospinal fluid leak) or purulent (bacterial sinusitis).
Episodes of intense, uncontrollable shivering accompanied by a rapid rise in body temperature, a classic sign of bacteremia or sepsis.
Endoscopic instrument used to examine the sigmoid colon and rectum for screening, biopsy, and polyp removal.
Wire loop deployed through an endoscope to grasp and remove polyps, tumors, or foreign bodies by electrosurgical or mechanical excision.
Relating to sensory signals from the body including touch, pain, temperature, and proprioception.
Device that measures the volume and flow rate of air inhaled and exhaled, used in pulmonary function testing to assess respiratory capacity.
Nerves supplying the abdominal and pelvic viscera, carrying sympathetic fibers that regulate gut motility and splanchnic vascular tone.
Surgical removal of the spleen, indicated for traumatic rupture, immune thrombocytopenia, or splenic malignancy.
Degenerative spinal changes including osteophyte formation and disc degeneration, a common cause of neck and back pain in older adults.
A medical instrument used to listen to internal body sounds, particularly heart and lungs.
Located or administered under the tongue, allowing rapid absorption directly into the bloodstream and bypassing first-pass metabolism.
Located above the gum line, as in supragingival calculus removed during routine dental scaling.
Exhibiting clinical signs or symptoms of disease, contrasted with asymptomatic presentation in which disease is present but undetected or unfelt.
Abnormally rapid breathing rate, a sign of respiratory distress, fever, metabolic acidosis, or anxiety.
Surgical incision into the chest wall to access the thoracic cavity.
Surgical or catheter-based removal of a blood clot, used urgently in ischemic stroke, deep vein thrombosis, and pulmonary embolism.
Clinical syndrome resulting from excess thyroid hormone action, presenting with weight loss, palpitations, tremor, and heat intolerance.
A generalized seizure characterized by a tonic (stiffening) phase followed by a clonic (rhythmic jerking) phase, formerly known as grand mal.
The study of body surface features and their relationships to underlying structures.
The study of the adverse effects of chemicals and drugs on living organisms, with clinical toxicology focused on managing poisoning and overdose.
Administered or occurring through the intact skin, as in transdermal patches that provide sustained systemic drug delivery.
Three-headed muscle on the posterior upper arm responsible for elbow extension, whose reflex tests radial nerve integrity.
A sudden, sharp, brief pain used to describe fleeting musculoskeletal or nerve-related discomfort.
A brief, involuntary contraction of a muscle or muscle group, which may be benign or a sign of neuromuscular disease.
Calcification and scarring of the tympanic membrane and middle ear structures from recurrent otitis media, which can cause conductive hearing loss.
Relating to the ulna, the medial forearm bone, alongside which the ulnar nerve courses to supply the hand.
Flexible vascular cord connecting the fetus to the placenta, containing two umbilical arteries and one umbilical vein.
Enlarged, tortuous superficial veins most commonly in the legs, caused by incompetent venous valves and venous hypertension.
Narrowing of blood vessel lumens by smooth muscle contraction, raising blood pressure and reducing peripheral blood flow.
Widening of blood vessel lumens by smooth muscle relaxation, lowering blood pressure and increasing tissue blood flow.
Sudden reversible contraction of a blood vessel that narrows its lumen, clinically significant in Raynaud disease and after subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Slowing or stagnation of venous blood flow, a major predisposing factor for deep vein thrombosis.
A machine mechanically assisting or replacing spontaneous breathing in patients who cannot breathe adequately.
Sensory receptors within internal organs detecting pressure, stretch, pain, and chemical changes.
Consciously controlled movement initiated by the motor cortex, impaired by upper motor neuron lesions affecting the corticospinal tract.
A pulse difficult to palpate due to reduced stroke volume or vasoconstriction, indicating shock or significant cardiac compromise.
Yellow discoloration of cerebrospinal fluid from hemoglobin breakdown, a key finding confirming subarachnoid hemorrhage when initial CT is negative.
Surgical reshaping and tightening of the abdominal wall by removing excess skin and fat, commonly performed after significant weight loss or pregnancy.
Surgical removal of one or both adrenal glands, performed for adrenal tumors, pheochromocytoma, or primary hyperaldosteronism.
Sampling of amniotic fluid by needle puncture for fetal genetic or developmental testing.
X-ray image of blood vessels obtained after injection of contrast dye.
Minimally invasive endovascular procedure to widen narrowed or obstructed vessels using a balloon catheter, with or without stent placement.
Radiographic imaging of the aorta using injected contrast dye.
X-ray imaging of arteries using contrast dye to detect blockages or abnormalities.
Direct visual examination of the airways using a flexible fiber-optic scope.
Surgical removal of one or more carpal (wrist) bones.
Radiographic imaging of the bile ducts using contrast dye.
Surgical incision into the common bile duct to remove stones or relieve obstruction.
Visual examination of the bladder and urethra using a cystoscope.
Endoscopic examination of the duodenum used to diagnose ulcers, tumors, and celiac disease, and to perform biopsies or therapeutic interventions.
Endoscopic examination of the small intestine used to detect bleeding, Crohn's disease, and tumors beyond the reach of standard upper endoscopy.
Direct visual examination of the esophagus using an endoscope.
Surgical removal or release of fascia, performed to treat Dupuytren's contracture or relieve compartment syndrome.
Surgical removal of all or part of the stomach, performed for gastric cancer or severe peptic ulcer disease.
Surgical removal of gum tissue to treat severe periodontal disease.
Direct visual examination of the uterine cavity using a hysteroscope.
Surgical creation of an opening from the ileum to the abdominal wall for waste elimination.
Minimally invasive procedure to restore vertebral height and relieve pain from compression fractures.
Surgical reshaping of the labia for functional or cosmetic purposes.
Visual examination of the larynx using a laryngoscope.
Low-dose X-ray image of breast tissue used for screening or diagnosis of breast cancer.
Surgical reshaping or reconstruction of the breast, encompassing augmentation, reduction, and post-mastectomy reconstruction.
Surgical incision of the eardrum to drain fluid from the middle ear.
Surgical crushing of a nerve to relieve chronic or intractable pain.
Surgical removal of one or both testes, performed for testicular cancer or as part of hormone therapy for prostate cancer.
Surgical removal of an ovary, performed for ovarian cysts, tumors, or torsion.
Imaging of the pancreatic ducts, typically via ERCP.
The puncture of a vein to collect blood for laboratory analysis, therapeutic bloodletting (as in polycythemia vera), or blood donation.
Visual examination of the rectum and anus using a rigid scope.
Radiographic imaging of the renal pelvis and urinary tract using contrast dye.
Surgical incision of the pyloric muscle to relieve infantile pyloric stenosis.
Surgical repair of a deviated nasal septum to improve airflow.
Visual examination of the sigmoid colon and rectum using a flexible scope.
Surgical incision into a paranasal sinus to drain infection or remove obstruction.
Surgical incision through the sternum to access the thoracic cavity.
Auscultatory examination of the chest using a stethoscope.
Surgical removal of all or part of the thyroid gland.
Imaging technique that generates cross-sectional images of the body.
Incision into the tracheal wall to create a surgical airway, performed for upper airway obstruction, prolonged ventilation, or failed intubation.
Surgical repair of the tympanic membrane, typically performed to close perforations caused by chronic infection or trauma and to restore hearing.
Visual examination of the ureter using a ureteroscope.
Surgical repair of the urethra for stricture or injury.
Surgical severing of the vas deferens for permanent male sterilization.
Radiographic or nuclear imaging of the cardiac or cerebral ventricles.
Surgical creation of an opening from the bladder to the skin surface.
Persistent, irrational fear of heights that triggers significant anxiety or avoidance behavior, classified as a specific phobia.
Inflammation of a lymph node or gland in response to regional bacterial or viral infection, presenting with local tenderness and swelling.
Anxiety disorder characterized by fear of open or crowded spaces from which escape is difficult.
A slightly movable joint connected by fibrocartilage; e.g., pubic symphysis.
Inability to sweat normally, impairing the body's ability to regulate temperature.
Stiffening and immobility of a joint due to fusion of bones or fibrous tissue.
Narrowing of the aortic valve opening, obstructing blood flow from the left ventricle.
Chronic inflammation of the eyelid margins, often caused by bacterial infection or seborrhea.
Drooping of the upper eyelid due to muscle weakness or nerve damage.
Inflammation of the cerebellum, often following viral infection.
Intense anxiety triggered by enclosed or confined spaces.
Inflammation of the colon caused by infection, inflammatory bowel disease, or ischemia, presenting with diarrhea, cramping, and rectal bleeding.
Inflammation of the cornea, also termed keratitis, caused by bacterial, viral, or fungal infection and a leading cause of corneal blindness worldwide.
Premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures in infants, restricting brain growth.
Infection and inflammation of the lacrimal sac, causing tearing and discharge.
Inflammation of the skin causing redness, itching, and rash, occurring in multiple forms including contact, atopic, and seborrheic dermatitis.
Inflammation of the duodenum, often caused by H. pylori or NSAIDs.
Inflammation of the small intestine, typically from infection or Crohn's disease.
Acute inflammation of the epiglottis, a life-threatening airway emergency.
Inflammation of the esophageal lining, most commonly from acid reflux or infection.
