Inflammation of the fascia, encompassing conditions ranging from plantar fasciitis to the life-threatening necrotizing fasciitis.
Chronic condition characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and cognitive disturbance.
Excessive formation of fibrous connective tissue as a reparative response to injury.
Inflammation of the gingiva (gums), the earliest stage of periodontal disease.
Scarring and hardening of the glomeruli, leading to progressive kidney failure.
Inflammation of the tongue causing swelling, color change, and altered texture.
Inflammation of the liver caused by viral infection (hepatitis A, B, or C), alcohol, medications, or autoimmune disease.
Excessive sweating beyond what is needed for thermoregulation.
Inflammation of the ileum, most commonly a manifestation of Crohn's disease but also caused by infection or ischemia.
Inflammation of the jejunum, often from Crohn's disease or infection.
Simultaneous inflammation of the jejunum and ileum, most commonly a manifestation of Crohn's disease, causing abdominal pain, diarrhea, and malabsorption.
Inflammation of the larynx causing hoarseness or voice loss.
Inflammation of the tympanic membrane, often accompanying otitis media.
Death of body tissue due to injury, infection, or impaired blood supply.
Abnormal downward displacement of the kidney when standing.
Fungal infection of the nails, causing thickening, discoloration, and brittleness.
Inflammation of one or both testes, typically from viral or bacterial infection.
Infection of bone, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
Inflammation of the ear, classified as otitis externa (outer ear canal) or otitis media (middle ear), each with distinct causes and management.
Inflammation of the pharynx causing sore throat, most commonly viral but sometimes caused by group A Streptococcus requiring antibiotic treatment.
Inflammation of a vein wall, often associated with clot formation.
Inflammation of the pleural lining causing sharp chest pain that worsens with breathing.
Viral infection affecting the gray matter of the spinal cord, potentially causing paralysis.
Inflammation of the rectal mucosa caused by inflammatory bowel disease, sexually transmitted infection, or radiation therapy.
Inflammation of the prostate gland classified as acute bacterial, chronic bacterial, or non-bacterial, presenting with pelvic pain and urinary symptoms.
Severe mental disorder involving loss of contact with reality, hallucinations, or delusions.
Narrowing of the pyloric outlet that obstructs gastric emptying, typically presenting in male infants with projectile vomiting and requiring surgical correction.
Impulse control disorder characterized by compulsive fire-setting driven by tension relief rather than material gain, classified under disruptive and impulse-control disorders.
Inflammation of the nasal mucosa causing congestion, runny nose, and sneezing.
Multisystem inflammatory disease characterized by non-caseating granulomas most commonly affecting the lungs, lymph nodes, skin, and eyes, often discovered incidentally on chest imaging.
Inflammation of the sigmoid colon caused by inflammatory bowel disease, ischemia, or infection, presenting with lower left abdominal pain and altered bowel habits.
Pain along the spine arising from muscular, neural, or vertebral pathology.
Inflammation of one or more vertebrae, most notably in ankylosing spondylitis, an immune-mediated condition causing progressive spinal fusion.
Inflammation of the synovial membrane lining a joint, causing pain and swelling.
Inflammation of a tendon, typically from overuse or repetitive strain.
Formation of a blood clot within a blood vessel, obstructing flow.
Inflammation of the tonsils, most often caused by viral or streptococcal infection.
Inflammation of the trachea, often of bacterial origin, causing stridor, barking cough, and respiratory distress.
Impulse control disorder involving compulsive urge to pull out one's own hair.
Inflammation of the urethra, commonly caused by sexually transmitted infections.
Formation of a blood clot within a vein, encompassing deep vein thrombosis and superficial thrombophlebitis.
Benign tumor arising from the vestibulocochlear nerve, causing hearing loss and tinnitus.
Benign epithelial tumor arising from glandular tissue, occurring in organs such as the thyroid, colon, and pituitary gland.
Catecholamine hormone secreted by the adrenal medulla that triggers the fight-or-flight response by increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and blood glucose.
Habitual swallowing of excess air, leading to bloating, belching, and abdominal discomfort.
Intestinal infection caused by the protozoan Entamoeba histolytica, ranging from asymptomatic colonization to dysentery and hepatic abscess.
Age-related gradual decline in testosterone levels in men, also termed late-onset hypogonadism, associated with fatigue, reduced libido, and mood changes.
Loss of sensation induced pharmacologically for surgical or diagnostic procedures.
Permanent focal dilation of the aortic wall, most common in the infrarenal abdominal segment, with rupture carrying a high mortality risk.
Complete cessation of breathing, classified as obstructive (airway blockage), central (absent respiratory drive), or mixed.
Surgical fusion of a joint to eliminate movement and relieve pain.
Wasting or reduction in size of a tissue or organ due to disuse, malnutrition, or disease.
Standardized testing of hearing sensitivity used to diagnose and classify hearing loss as conductive, sensorineural, or mixed.
Pertaining to a condition in which the immune system attacks the body's own tissues.
Post-mortem examination of a body to determine cause of death or study disease.
Presence of viable bacteria in the bloodstream, a condition that may progress to sepsis if the immune response is inadequate.
Radiation therapy delivered by placing a radioactive source directly inside or near a tumor.
Slowness of voluntary movement, a cardinal feature of Parkinson's disease and other parkinsonian disorders.
Narrowing of the bronchial airways from smooth muscle contraction, the primary mechanism underlying airflow obstruction in asthma.
Widening of the bronchial airways to improve airflow, achieved by bronchodilator medications or as a physiological response to sympathetic stimulation.
Sudden involuntary contraction of bronchial smooth muscle causing wheezing and dyspnea.
A high-pitched whistling respiratory sound caused by narrowed airways, associated with asthma, COPD, and anaphylaxis.
Low-pitched rattling breath sounds heard on auscultation from secretions or partial obstruction in larger airways, often clearing with coughing.
Physician specializing in the diagnosis and treatment of cardiovascular disease.
Restoration of normal cardiac rhythm using synchronized electrical shock or medication.
Compression of the median nerve at the wrist causing numbness, tingling, and weakness.
Group of permanent movement and posture disorders caused by brain damage before or during birth.
Benign tumor composed of mature cartilage tissue, arising within bone (enchondroma) or on its surface (ecchondroma), most commonly in the small bones of the hand.
Softening and deterioration of cartilage, most commonly under the kneecap.
The process by which blood forms clots to stop bleeding.
Persistent pain in the coccyx (tailbone) region, often following trauma.
Surgical replacement of a damaged or diseased cornea with donor tissue.
Small signaling protein secreted by immune cells that regulates immune responses.
Removal of dead, damaged, or infected tissue to promote healing of a wound.
Deficit of body water causing electrolyte imbalance and reduced physiological function.
Healthcare professional specializing in the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of oral disease.
Physician specializing in diseases of the skin, hair, and nails.
Medical procedure that filters waste, excess fluid, and toxins from blood when kidneys fail.
Frequent passage of loose or watery stools, a common symptom of gastrointestinal infection, inflammatory bowel disease, and other digestive disorders.
Involuntary abnormal movements, commonly occurring as a complication of long-term levodopa therapy in Parkinson's disease.
Impaired ability to produce or comprehend language due to brain damage.
Difficulty or abnormality in voice production caused by laryngeal disorders, vocal cord lesions, or nerve damage, assessed by laryngoscopy.
Chronic lung disease with permanent alveolar destruction, reducing gas exchange surface.
The inner mucous membrane lining the uterine cavity, which thickens under hormonal influence and is shed during menstruation.
Hormone produced by the kidneys stimulating red blood cell production in bone marrow.
A weaker form of estrogen measured in pregnancy screening for fetal abnormalities.
Pertaining to glands that secrete substances through ducts onto body surfaces.
Abnormal protrusion of one or both eyeballs from the orbit, most commonly associated with Graves' disease and thyroid-associated orbitopathy.
Pancreatic hormone that raises blood glucose by stimulating hepatic glycogen breakdown.
Presence of glucose in the urine, typically indicating uncontrolled diabetes mellitus or a lowered renal glucose threshold.
Iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from lungs to tissues.
Destruction of red blood cells with release of hemoglobin into circulation.
Malignant tumor arising from hepatocytes, also termed hepatocellular carcinoma, the most common primary liver cancer and a major complication of cirrhosis.
Describing a substance capable of causing damage to liver cells, including alcohol, acetaminophen in overdose, and medications such as isoniazid.
Surgical repair of a hernia by suturing the defect in the abdominal wall.
Severe, uncontrollable vomiting, as in hyperemesis gravidarum which occurs in early pregnancy and can cause dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.
Abnormally increased sensitivity to sensory stimuli including touch, pain, or temperature.
Refractive error in which distant objects are seen more clearly than near objects.
Abnormally increased muscle tone, presenting as spasticity from upper motor neuron lesions or rigidity from basal ganglia disease such as Parkinson's.
Abnormally low potassium concentration in the blood, causing muscle weakness, cramps, and potentially life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias.
Dangerously low core body temperature that impairs normal physiological function.
State of impaired immune function leaving the body susceptible to infection.
Malignant proliferation of white blood cell precursors in bone marrow and blood.
Abnormally low white blood cell count, increasing susceptibility to infection.
Benign soft tissue tumor composed of mature adipocytes, typically presenting as a painless, mobile subcutaneous mass.
Endoscopic crushing of a bladder stone followed by irrigation to remove fragments.
Cancer of lymphatic tissue, broadly classified as Hodgkin or non-Hodgkin lymphoma, presenting with lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms.
Flat, circumscribed area of skin discoloration that is not raised or palpable, distinct from a papule by the absence of surface elevation.
Expectoration of black or dark-colored sputum, a characteristic finding in coal workers' pneumoconiosis.
Usually benign, slow-growing tumor arising from the meninges.
Protrusion of the meninges through a defect in the skull or spine.
Permanent cessation of menstruation marking the end of female reproductive capacity.
Abnormally heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding caused by fibroids, coagulopathy, or hormonal imbalance, often significantly impairing quality of life.
Abnormally small head circumference due to impaired brain development.
Disease or dysfunction of the spinal cord caused by disc compression, demyelination, or ischemia, presenting with limb weakness and sensory disturbance.
Oxygen-binding heme protein in muscle tissue released into the bloodstream after muscle injury, serving as a marker of rhabdomyolysis.
Disease of the muscle characterized by weakness and wasting.
Refractive error in which near objects are seen clearly but distant objects are blurred.
Chronic neurological disorder causing uncontrollable episodes of daytime sleepiness.
Any new and abnormal growth of tissue, classified as benign or malignant based on cell type, origin, and invasive behavior.
Surgical fixation of a prolapsed kidney to the posterior abdominal wall.
Describing a substance capable of causing renal tubular or glomerular damage, including NSAIDs, aminoglycoside antibiotics, and iodinated contrast agents.
Malignant tumor arising from immature sympathetic nerve cells, the most common extracranial solid cancer in young children.
Physician specializing in disorders of the nervous system.
The most abundant circulating white blood cell and a key effector of innate immunity, recruited early to sites of infection or tissue injury.
Abnormally low sperm concentration in semen, a common cause of male factor infertility.
Instrument used to examine the interior of the eye, including the retina and optic disc.
Surgical fixation of an undescended testicle into the scrotum.
Dental specialty focused on correcting misaligned teeth and jaws.
Softening of bones due to impaired mineralization, often from vitamin D deficiency.
Fluid-filled sac on or within the ovary, most of which are benign and resolve spontaneously, though some require monitoring or intervention.
Swelling of the optic disc caused by raised intracranial pressure.
Paralysis of the lower body and legs due to spinal cord injury or disease.
Four small endocrine glands adjacent to the thyroid that regulate calcium and phosphate.
Infection of the female upper reproductive tract including the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries, most commonly a complication of sexually transmitted infections.
Licensed healthcare professional who prepares, dispenses, and counsels on medications.
Sharp pain in the chest or abdomen from spasm of intercostal or pleural muscles.
Inflammatory infection of the lung parenchyma caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi.
Group of metabolic disorders caused by enzyme defects in heme biosynthesis.
Abnormal enlargement of the prostate gland caused by benign prostatic hyperplasia or malignancy, potentially obstructing urinary flow.
Collection of fluid resembling a true cyst but lacking an epithelial lining, most commonly occurring as a complication of acute pancreatitis.
Bacterial skin infection producing pus-filled lesions, with common forms including impetigo and ecthyma, while pyoderma gangrenosum may signal underlying systemic disease.
Elevated body temperature above the normal range, used as a clinical synonym for fever.
Substance that induces fever by acting on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center.
Presence of white blood cells in the urine, indicating urinary tract infection or urinary tract inflammation.
Complete paralysis of all four limbs due to high cervical spinal cord injury.
Protrusion of the rectum through the anal opening.
Herniation of the posterior vaginal wall with protrusion of the rectum.
Return or worsening of a disease after a period of improvement or remission.
Surgical removal of all or part of an organ or tissue.
Separation of the retina from the underlying retinal pigment epithelium.
Non-inflammatory disease of the retina, most commonly caused by diabetes mellitus or hypertension, potentially leading to vision loss.
Bloodstream infection characterized by proliferating bacteria, a clinical term now largely replaced by the broader concept of sepsis.
Excessive drowsiness or sleepiness, which may indicate central nervous system depression, metabolic disorder, or medication side effect.
Device used to measure arterial blood pressure.
Pulmonary function test measuring the volume and flow of air inhaled and exhaled.
Forward slipping of one vertebra over the one below, causing back pain and nerve compression.
Junction between two neurons where nerve impulses are transmitted chemically or electrically.
Viscous lubricating fluid secreted by the synovial membrane within joint cavities.
Dilation of small superficial blood vessels forming visible red lines on the skin.
Surgical anchoring of a tendon to bone to restore function or stability.
Instrument for measuring body temperature.
Pharmacological dissolution of a blood clot using thrombolytic agents.
Instrument used to measure intraocular pressure for glaucoma screening.
Presence of bacterial toxins or metabolic waste products in the bloodstream.
Relating to drug administration through the skin for systemic effect.
Transfer of blood or blood components from a donor into a recipient's bloodstream.
Having three cusps, as in the tricuspid valve separating the right atrium and right ventricle, which prevents backflow during ventricular contraction.
Each of the three successive three-month periods of a pregnancy.
Diagnostic test measuring the movement of the tympanic membrane in response to air pressure.
Protrusion of abdominal contents through a defect at the navel.
Presence of calculi within the ureter causing severe flank pain (renal colic) and potentially obstructing urine flow, treated with ureteroscopy or lithotripsy.
Formation of stones within the urinary tract — kidneys, ureters, or bladder — causing flank pain, hematuria, and potentially obstructing urine flow.
Abnormal enlargement of the pampiniform venous plexus within the scrotum.
Puncture of a vein with a needle to obtain blood or administer treatment.
Life-threatening arrhythmia with chaotic ventricular electrical activity and no effective output.
Abnormal backflow of urine from the bladder into the ureters.
Abnormal enlargement of one or more abdominal organs.
Yellow discoloration of the skin, often from carotenemia or jaundice.
Yellow fatty deposit under the skin caused by elevated blood lipids.
Presence of albumin in the urine, an early and sensitive marker of glomerular damage and chronic kidney disease progression.
Archaic term for iron-deficiency anemia causing greenish-yellow skin pallor.
Light-sensitive pigment in retinal rod cells essential for vision in low-light conditions.
Adjacent to the glomerulus, as in the juxtaglomerular cells that regulate blood pressure by secreting renin into the circulation.
The largest of the eight carpal bones, located at the center of the wrist.
Study of biological rhythms and their effects on physiology, behavior, and health.
Pertaining to the visceral organs of the abdomen, as in the splanchnic nerves that supply the abdominal and pelvic organs.
Normal left-sided positioning of the heart, a term used clinically when situs inversus affects other thoracic or abdominal organs.
Congenital condition where the heart is positioned on the right side of the chest.
Describing any procedure or drug delivery method that passes through the skin rather than through a natural body opening.
Refractive error from an irregularly curved cornea or lens causing uneven light focus and blurred vision at all distances.
Ring of smooth muscle at the stomach-duodenum junction regulating passage of partially digested food into the small intestine.
The lower esophageal sphincter at the gastroesophageal junction that prevents reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus.
A hormone secreted by the pineal gland that regulates the sleep-wake cycle in response to light and darkness.
Pituitary hormone that promotes renal water reabsorption to reduce urine output and regulate plasma osmolality, also called vasopressin.
Major peripheral nerve of the upper limb supplying sensation to the palm and motor function to the thenar muscles, compressed in carpal tunnel syndrome.
Any substance, typically a protein, that the immune system recognizes as foreign and mounts a specific immune response against.
A protein (immunoglobulin) produced by B lymphocytes that binds specifically to an antigen to neutralize it or mark it for destruction.
Cerebral lobe posterior to the frontal lobe that processes sensory input including touch, pressure, temperature, and spatial awareness.
A small endocrine gland in the brain that secretes melatonin, regulating circadian rhythms and sleep-wake cycles.
The mature female gamete released from the ovary during ovulation, which develops into an embryo if fertilized by a spermatozoon.
Small anatomical sac, referring either to ovarian follicles containing developing oocytes or to hair follicles from which hairs emerge.
One of the paired male gonads located in the scrotum, responsible for producing spermatozoa and secreting testosterone.
The muscular duct that transports sperm from the epididymis to the ejaculatory duct during ejaculation.
A drug class that reduces pain, fever, and inflammation by inhibiting COX enzymes; ibuprofen (Advil), naproxen (Aleve), and celecoxib (Celebrex) are common examples.
Firm belief in being pregnant accompanied by physical symptoms such as abdominal distension and amenorrhea, despite the absence of a fetus.
The largest and uppermost bone of the pelvis, forming the prominent hip structure palpable on each side of the body.
A digital version of a patient's medical history, diagnoses, medications, and treatment records, maintained by a healthcare provider.
Medication available without a prescription, approved for self-treatment of minor conditions and sold directly to consumers.
Medication requiring a licensed prescriber's authorization, used for conditions that necessitate medical diagnosis and supervision.
Paired duct extending from each side of the uterus toward the ovary, serving as the site of fertilization and the pathway for the ovum to the uterus.
Muscular canal extending from the cervix to the external genitalia, serving as the birth canal during delivery and the passage for menstrual flow.
The collective term for the external female genitalia, including the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, and vaginal opening.
A coiled tube located on the posterior surface of each testis where sperm mature and are stored before ejaculation.
The male external reproductive and urinary organ through which urine and semen are expelled from the body.
One of a pair of glands located posterior to the bladder that secretes fructose-rich fluid contributing to seminal fluid volume.
Anatomical plane that divides the body into left and right portions, with the midsagittal plane passing precisely through the body's midline.
Anatomical plane that divides the body into anterior and posterior portions, also called the coronal plane.
Anatomical plane that divides the body into superior and inferior portions, forming the basis of cross-sectional imaging such as CT.
The anatomical region corresponding to the upper arm, between the shoulder and elbow.
The anatomical region pertaining to the head.
The anatomical region of the neck, also referring to the cervical spine comprising the uppermost vertebrae from the skull to the thorax.
The anatomical region corresponding to the leg (shin), between the knee and ankle.
The bony cavity formed by the skull that houses and protects the brain and its coverings (meninges).
The canal formed by the vertebral column that encloses and protects the spinal cord.
The body cavity enclosed by the rib cage and diaphragm, containing the heart, lungs, and major vessels.
The combined abdominal and pelvic cavities below the diaphragm, housing the digestive, reproductive, and urinary organs.
Serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering most abdominal organs, whose inflammation constitutes peritonitis.
One of four clinical abdominal regions — RUQ, RLQ, LUQ, LLQ — used to localize pain, organs, and examination findings.
One of the nine clinical divisions of the abdomen used to describe the location of organs and symptoms.
Directional term indicating a position closer to the midline of the body.
Directional term indicating a position farther from the midline of the body.
Directional term indicating a position closer to the point of attachment or origin of a limb.
Directional term indicating a position farther from the point of attachment or origin of a limb.
The pointed tip of an anatomical structure, clinically applied to the cardiac apex at the inferior-left chest and the superior tip of each lung.
The deepest epidermal layer where keratinocytes actively divide and melanocytes reside, responsible for continuous skin cell renewal.
Pigment-producing cell in the basal epidermis that synthesizes melanin, giving skin its color and providing protection against UV radiation.
Tubular skin structure from which a hair grows, associated with sebaceous glands and arrector pili muscles that cause the hair to erect.
Exocrine skin gland that produces sweat, classified as eccrine (distributed widely across the skin) or apocrine (concentrated in the axillary and groin regions).
A gland in the skin that secretes sebum into hair follicles to lubricate and waterproof the skin and hair.
Specialized nerve endings in the skin and other tissues that detect stimuli such as touch, pressure, pain, temperature, and vibration.
Hard keratinized plate covering the dorsal surface of the distal phalanges, protecting the fingertips and aiding fine manipulation.
The pale, crescent-shaped area at the base of a nail, representing the visible portion of the nail matrix.
The thin fold of skin at the base of the nail that seals and protects the nail matrix from infection.
The oily substance secreted by sebaceous glands that lubricates the skin and hair and has mild antimicrobial properties.
Chronic inflammatory condition of the pilosebaceous unit producing comedones, papules, pustules, and nodules, most prevalent during adolescence.
Pertaining to or affecting the skin.
A superficial wound caused by friction that removes the outer layers of skin without penetrating the dermis.
A large fluid-filled skin blister, seen in burns, pemphigus vulgaris, and severe contact dermatitis.
A plugged hair follicle filled with sebum and dead cells, presenting as an open comedone (blackhead) or a closed comedone (whitehead).
Soft tissue injury causing hemorrhage beneath intact skin, resulting in discoloration and tenderness, commonly called a bruise.
A closed, epithelium-lined sac containing fluid, semifluid, or solid material, occurring in many tissues and ranging from benign to malignant.
Partial or complete loss of skin pigment (melanin), resulting in lighter or white patches.
Profuse, abnormal sweating occurring without physical exertion, indicating fever, shock, hypoglycemia, or autonomic dysfunction.
Severe inflammatory skin condition causing widespread redness and scaling across most of the body surface, also called exfoliative dermatitis.
Dry, leathery crust of necrotic tissue forming over a wound or burn that must be debrided to allow healing.
Narrow linear crack in the skin extending into the dermis, commonly occurring on the heels, fingertips, and lips.
Excess blood in a tissue or organ causing redness and warmth, classified as active (increased arterial inflow) or passive (venous congestion).
Darkening of localized skin areas from excess melanin production, caused by sun exposure, hormonal changes, or post-inflammatory pigmentation.
Any abnormal change in tissue structure caused by disease or injury, a broad term encompassing wounds, sores, tumors, and rashes.
Localized loss of skin pigmentation resulting in white patches, occurring as an idiopathic condition or associated with systemic disease.
A benign proliferation of melanocytes forming a pigmented skin lesion that may undergo malignant transformation into melanoma if certain changes occur.
A solid, raised skin lesion extending deeper into the dermis or subcutaneous tissue than a papule.
Abnormal softening of the nails, often associated with nutritional deficiency or chemical exposure.
Abnormal paleness of the skin, mucous membranes, or nail beds, often indicating anemia, shock, or poor perfusion.
A small, solid, raised skin lesion without visible fluid, seen in acne, eczema, and psoriasis.
Abnormal or exaggerated skin reaction to sunlight or UV radiation, occurring as a drug-induced effect or in autoimmune conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus.
Containing, consisting of, or producing pus, indicating an active bacterial infection with neutrophil accumulation.
A small, raised skin lesion filled with pus, common in acne, folliculitis, and impetigo.
Chronic autoimmune disease causing progressive fibrosis and hardening of the skin and internal organs, formerly known as scleroderma.
Excessive secretion of sebum from the sebaceous glands resulting in oily skin and scalp, associated with seborrheic dermatitis.
Characterized by the production of pus, describing infections or inflammatory processes involving pus formation.
Open sore on skin or mucous membrane resulting from tissue breakdown caused by infection, pressure, ischemia, or chronic inflammation.
A small, fluid-filled skin blister seen in herpes simplex, chickenpox, and contact dermatitis.
A transient, raised, pale skin lesion surrounded by erythema caused by localized dermal edema, the primary lesion of urticaria.
Abnormal dryness of the skin from decreased sebum production or impaired skin barrier function, also termed xerosis cutis.
A localized collection of pus within a cavity formed by tissue breakdown, surrounded by inflamed tissue, typically requiring incision and drainage.
Chronic facial skin condition with persistent redness, visible blood vessels, and acne-like papules affecting the central face.
Common acne involving comedones, papules, pustules, and nodules from sebaceous gland inflammation and Cutibacterium acnes infection.
A congenital absence or deficiency of melanin pigment in the skin, hair, and eyes due to genetic defects in melanin synthesis.
The most common skin cancer, arising from epidermal basal cells and strongly associated with chronic UV exposure, locally invasive but rarely metastasizing.
Tissue injury caused by heat, chemicals, electricity, or radiation, classified by depth of tissue destruction and extent of body surface involvement.
A scar formed by fibrous connective tissue replacing normal skin after wound healing.
Skin and tissue injury from sustained pressure over bony prominences, also called a pressure ulcer or bedsore, occurring in immobile patients.
A general term for any non-inflammatory skin disease or disorder.
Death of body tissue from loss of blood supply or severe bacterial infection, classified as dry (ischemic) or wet (infectious) based on the mechanism.
Ischemic necrosis of tissue without bacterial involvement, typically affecting the extremities, where the tissue gradually desiccates and mummifies.
Necrosis of tissue complicated by bacterial infection, producing a moist, malodorous wound with rapid tissue destruction.
Chronic inflammatory skin condition causing itching, redness, and a scaly rash, often used interchangeably with atopic dermatitis.
A group of genetic skin disorders characterized by dry, thickened, scaly skin resembling fish scales, ranging from mild to severe forms.
Contagious superficial bacterial skin infection caused by Staphylococcus or Streptococcus, presenting with characteristic honey-colored crusted lesions.
Malignant vascular tumor of the skin and mucous membranes caused by human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8), strongly associated with HIV/AIDS and immunosuppression.
Excessive fibrous scar tissue growing beyond the original wound margins, more prevalent in darker skin types and causing itching or pain.
Abnormal thickening of the skin's outer keratin layer, encompassing actinic keratosis (precancerous) and seborrheic keratosis (benign).
An irregular tear or cut in the skin or soft tissue caused by trauma, typically requiring wound closure and tetanus prophylaxis.
Aggressive malignant tumor arising from melanocytes, the most dangerous skin cancer due to its high propensity for early metastasis.
Infestation of the skin or hair by parasitic lice, causing intense itching and transmitted through close personal contact or shared items.
Contagious viral infection presenting with a fine pink rash, dangerous during pregnancy as it causes congenital rubella syndrome with serious fetal defects.
Contagious skin infestation by Sarcoptes scabiei mites, causing intense nocturnal itching and a papular rash, especially in skin folds.
A benign, slow-growing cyst beneath the skin formed by the accumulation of keratin and sebum within a follicular sac.
Malignant tumor arising from epidermal keratinocytes, associated with UV exposure and immunosuppression, with metastatic potential if left untreated.
Benign vascular birthmark from proliferating blood vessels, appearing in infancy and typically resolving spontaneously by early childhood.
Chronic systemic autoimmune disease affecting multiple organs, with hallmarks including a butterfly facial rash, joint pain, and renal involvement.
Superficial dermatophyte fungal infection of skin, hair, or nails, named according to the affected body site, such as tinea capitis on the scalp or tinea pedis on the feet.
Dermatophyte fungal infection of the scalp and hair shafts, predominantly affecting children and potentially causing patchy hair loss.
Common dermatophyte infection of the feet, particularly between the toes, characterized by itching, scaling, and fissures.
Primary varicella-zoster virus infection presenting with a widespread itchy vesicular rash, with potentially severe complications in immunocompromised patients.
Benign epidermal growth caused by human papillomavirus, with types including verruca vulgaris (common wart) and verruca plantaris (plantar wart).
An autoimmune condition causing progressive depigmentation of the skin in irregular patches due to destruction of melanocytes.
Deep skin infection consisting of clustered, interconnected furuncles caused by Staphylococcus aureus, forming a large, painful, pus-filled mass.
Deep bacterial infection of a hair follicle and surrounding tissue caused by Staphylococcus aureus, forming a painful, pus-filled nodule.
Corynebacterium infection of axillary or pubic hair shafts, characterized by yellowish, reddish, or black concretions adhering to the hair.
Inflammation of the nail matrix, often causing pain, swelling, and nail deformity.
Compulsive biting or chewing of the nails, occurring as a habitual behavior or in association with anxiety disorders.
Infection of the skin surrounding the nail causing redness, swelling, and pus formation, classified as acute (bacterial) or chronic (fungal).
Laboratory test that cultures a specimen to identify the causative organism and determines which antibiotics it is susceptible to.
Surgical removal of an entire lesion along with a margin of surrounding tissue for histopathological examination.
Microscopic examination of cells shed from a body surface or obtained by scraping, used to detect malignancy, infection, or other cellular abnormalities.
Rapid intraoperative technique in which snap-frozen tissue is sectioned and examined microscopically to guide surgical decisions.
Diagnostic procedure scraping skin, nail, or hair samples for microscopic examination or culture to identify fungal infection.
Tissue graft from a genetically non-identical donor of the same species, used as biological wound coverage in burns and reconstructive surgery.
A tissue graft harvested from and transplanted to the same individual, regarded as the gold standard for permanent skin replacement.
A surgical instrument that cuts thin skin slices for grafting, and also the term for the skin area innervated by a single spinal nerve root.
Surgical repair or reconstruction of the skin, typically using grafting techniques.
Transplantation of healthy skin to cover a wound where skin has been lost due to burns, trauma, or surgery.
A tissue graft taken from a donor of a different species, such as porcine skin, used as a temporary biological wound covering.
Destruction of tissue using heat, cold, electricity, or chemicals to stop bleeding, remove lesions, or prevent infection.
Destruction of abnormal tissue using extreme cold, typically applied with liquid nitrogen, to treat warts, actinic keratoses, and superficial skin cancers.
Removal of tissue from a body cavity or surface using a curette, a spoon-shaped instrument, used to sample or ablate skin lesions.
Use of a high-frequency electric current to cut tissue or coagulate blood vessels to control bleeding during surgery.
A minor surgical procedure to open and drain a skin abscess or infected cyst, relieving pain and facilitating healing.
Surgical removal of all or part of a nail, performed for severe infection, ingrown nail, or nail unit tumor.
Application of a chemical peel to remove outer skin layers and promote resurfacing, used to treat acne scars, fine lines, and pigmentation irregularities.
Mechanical skin resurfacing using a rotating abrasive device to remove superficial skin layers, used for scars, wrinkles, and tattoo removal.
Use of focused light energy to treat skin conditions including vascular lesions, pigmentation, scars, hair removal, and skin resurfacing.
A surgical procedure that removes subcutaneous fat deposits by suction through small incisions, used for body contouring.
Surgical removal of excess facial skin and tightening of underlying tissues to reduce the appearance of wrinkles and facial sagging.
A drug that produces reversible loss of sensation (local) or consciousness (general); lidocaine (Xylocaine), propofol (Diprivan), and sevoflurane (Ultane) are common agents.
A drug that kills or inhibits fungal growth; fluconazole (Diflucan), terbinafine (Lamisil), and clotrimazole (Lotrimin) are commonly used agents.
A drug acting against parasites including protozoa, helminths, and ectoparasites; metronidazole (Flagyl), ivermectin (Stromectol), and albendazole (Albenza) are key agents.
An agent that relieves or prevents itching, including antihistamines, topical corticosteroids, and soothing preparations such as calamine lotion.
A substance applied to living tissue to prevent or inhibit the growth of microorganisms and reduce the risk of infection.
A topical corticosteroid preparation that reduces skin inflammation, itching, and redness; hydrocortisone (Cortaid), triamcinolone (Kenalog), and betamethasone (Diprolene) are common formulations.
Toward the front of the body, opposite of posterior.
Toward the back of the body, opposite of anterior.
Toward the head or upper part of the body, opposite of inferior.
Toward the feet or lower part of the body, opposite of superior.
The anterior lobe of the cerebral cortex responsible for executive function, voluntary movement, speech production (Broca's area), and personality.
The lateral lobe of the cerebral cortex responsible for auditory processing, language comprehension (Wernicke's area), and memory formation.
The posterior lobe of the cerebral cortex dedicated to visual processing, whose damage causes visual field defects or cortical blindness.
Central relay station of the brain that routes sensory and motor signals to the cerebral cortex and plays a role in regulating consciousness and alertness.
Small but vital brain structure regulating hormone secretion via the pituitary gland, body temperature, hunger, thirst, and sleep cycles.
The two-leaflet valve between the left atrium and left ventricle, whose dysfunction causes mitral regurgitation or mitral stenosis.
The artery carrying deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs, the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood.
The bony structure encasing and protecting the brain, formed by the calvarium above and the skull base below.
Bowl-shaped bony structure at the base of the spine connecting the vertebral column to the lower limbs, supporting the abdominal organs and forming the birth canal in females.
The point of connection between two or more bones, classified as fibrous, cartilaginous, or synovial based on structure and degree of movement permitted.
The vascular organ that develops during pregnancy to supply the fetus with oxygen and nutrients via the umbilical cord, also producing hormones that sustain the pregnancy.
The three-leaflet semilunar valve between the left ventricle and aorta, whose stenosis is the most common valvular heart disease requiring intervention in adults.
The semilunar valve between the right ventricle and pulmonary artery that prevents backflow from the pulmonary circulation into the right heart.
The veins returning oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium, the only veins in the body that carry oxygenated blood.
The major paired arteries supplying the head and neck, with the internal carotid supplying the brain and the external carotid supplying the face, and a common site of atherosclerosis.
The major artery of the thigh, commonly used as a vascular access site for cardiac catheterization and peripheral vascular procedures.
The longest peripheral nerve in the body, running from the lower back through the buttock and down each leg, whose compression by a herniated disc causes sciatica.
The tenth cranial nerve controlling parasympathetic function of the heart, lungs, and digestive tract, and involved in the vasovagal reflex.
The second cranial nerve transmitting visual signals from the retina to the brain, whose compression or inflammation causes visual field defects or vision loss.
The seventh cranial nerve controlling facial muscle movement and taste sensation, whose damage causes the characteristic facial weakness of Bell's palsy.
The four-muscle group on the anterior thigh responsible for knee extension, whose integrity is assessed via the patellar tendon reflex.
The three-muscle group on the posterior thigh responsible for knee flexion and hip extension, commonly strained in athletic activity.
The lateral forearm bone on the thumb side, frequently fractured in falls on an outstretched hand in the classic Colles' fracture pattern.
The medial forearm bone whose proximal end forms the olecranon (point of the elbow) and articulates with the humerus at the elbow joint.
The slender lateral bone of the lower leg that provides muscle attachment and ankle stability while bearing minimal body weight.
The lower jaw bone and the only movable bone of the skull, essential for mastication, swallowing, and speech.
The muscular organ in the oral cavity essential for taste, mastication, swallowing, and speech, whose inflammation is termed glossitis.
The glandular tissue on the anterior chest wall that produces milk in females, and the most common site of cancer in women worldwide.
Exocrine glands producing saliva to lubricate the oral cavity and initiate digestion, with three major pairs: parotid, submandibular, and sublingual.
The terminal opening of the gastrointestinal tract through which stool is expelled, controlled by the internal and external anal sphincters.
The outermost grey matter layer of the cerebrum responsible for higher cognition, sensory perception, voluntary motor commands, and language.
The deepest layer of the skin below the dermis, composed mainly of adipose tissue and providing insulation, cushioning, and energy storage.
A group of subcortical nuclei coordinating motor control, habit formation, and reward processing, whose dysfunction causes movement disorders such as Parkinson's disease.
The large bundle of nerve fibres connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres, enabling interhemispheric communication.
The major veins draining blood from the head and neck, with jugular venous pressure assessed clinically to estimate right heart filling pressure.
The artery on the thumb side of the forearm, used for pulse measurement, arterial blood gas sampling, and coronary catheterization access.
The largest joint in the body, formed by the femur, tibia, and patella and stabilized by the cruciate and collateral ligaments and the menisci.
The ball-and-socket joint between the femoral head and the acetabulum, a common site of osteoarthritis and fragility fracture in older adults.
The ball-and-socket joint between the humeral head and the glenoid fossa of the scapula, the most mobile and the most frequently dislocated joint in the body.
Affecting or relating to both sides of the body or a paired organ, opposite of unilateral.
Relating to the back or posterior surface of the body, as in the dorsal surface of the hand which faces away from the palm.
Relating to the front or anterior surface of the body, used interchangeably with anterior in standard human anatomical descriptions.
Located close to or on the surface of the body, opposite of deep.
Dense connective tissue that surrounds and separates muscles, nerves, and organs, involved in compartment syndrome and conditions such as plantar fasciitis.
One of 12 pairs of curved bones forming the thoracic cage, protecting the heart and lungs and classified as true, false, or floating based on sternal attachment.
A chronic psychotic disorder characterized by hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking, and negative symptoms such as flat affect and social withdrawal.
A mood disorder characterized by alternating episodes of mania or hypomania and depression, formerly called manic-depressive illness.
A psychiatric disorder triggered by experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event, marked by intrusive memories, avoidance, hyperarousal, and negative mood changes.
A neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity that impairs daily functioning, commonly diagnosed in childhood.
A disorder characterized by unwanted recurring thoughts (obsessions) and repetitive behaviors (compulsions) performed to reduce anxiety.
A neurodevelopmental condition characterized by differences in social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors, with severity varying widely across individuals.
Recurrent thoughts of ending one's own life, ranging from passive death wishes to active planning, requiring immediate psychiatric assessment.
A sensory perception experienced without an external stimulus, occurring in any sensory modality and associated with psychosis, substance use, or neurological conditions.
A fixed false belief held with conviction despite contradictory evidence, a core feature of psychotic disorders such as schizophrenia.
An anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent unexpected panic attacks — sudden surges of intense fear — often accompanied by avoidance behavior.
A class of medications treating depression and anxiety disorders by modulating neurotransmitter activity; fluoxetine (Prozac), sertraline (Zoloft), and escitalopram (Lexapro) are first-line SSRIs.
A class of medications that treat psychotic symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions; olanzapine (Zyprexa), risperidone (Risperdal), and quetiapine (Seroquel) are commonly prescribed agents.
A class of medications that reduce the frequency and severity of mood episodes in bipolar disorder; lithium (Lithobid), valproate (Depakote), and lamotrigine (Lamictal) are key agents.
A structured psychotherapy that identifies and modifies maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors, with strong evidence across mood, anxiety, and eating disorders.
A mental health condition characterized by persistent disturbances in eating behavior and body image that significantly impair physical health or psychosocial functioning.
A pattern of substance use causing significant impairment or distress, characterized by loss of control, craving, tolerance, and withdrawal.
A distinct period of abnormally elevated or expansive mood with increased energy, decreased need for sleep, and impaired judgment, a defining feature of bipolar disorder.
A personality disorder characterized by unstable relationships, self-image, and emotions, along with impulsivity and recurrent self-harm or suicidal behavior.
Deliberate injury to one's own body without suicidal intent, most commonly by cutting or burning, used as a maladaptive coping mechanism for emotional distress.
A structured clinical assessment of a patient's psychological functioning covering appearance, behavior, speech, mood, affect, thought content, cognition, and insight.
A broad category of therapeutic approaches involving structured conversations between a therapist and patient to address mental health conditions and emotional difficulties.
An intense, persistent, and irrational fear of a specific object or situation that provokes anxiety and leads to avoidance behavior.
A disruption in the normally integrated experience of consciousness, identity, memory, or perception, ranging from mild detachment to dissociative identity disorder.
The observable outward expression of a person's emotional state, assessed in terms of range, intensity, and appropriateness to context.
An eating disorder characterized by restricted food intake, intense fear of weight gain, and a distorted body image, leading to significantly low body weight.
An eating disorder characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors such as purging, fasting, or excessive exercise.
A psychiatric treatment delivering a controlled electrical current through the brain under general anesthesia to induce a brief seizure, used for severe or treatment-resistant depression.
A condition in which a person experiences significant distress from physical symptoms that cannot be fully explained by a medical diagnosis.
A group of mental health conditions characterized by enduring maladaptive patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving that deviate from cultural expectations and cause significant distress.
A repetitive behavior or mental act performed in response to an obsession or rigid rules to reduce anxiety, a core feature of obsessive-compulsive disorder.
The outer layer of the adrenal gland that produces steroid hormones including cortisol, aldosterone, and androgens essential for metabolism and stress response.
A rare malignant tumor arising from the adrenal cortex that may secrete excess steroid hormones and carries a poor prognosis due to late presentation.
Subjective sensation of dry mouth resulting from reduced or absent salivary flow, associated with medications, radiation therapy, and Sjogren's syndrome.
A persistent urge to defecate or urinate with the passage of little or no material, associated with colorectal inflammation, infection, or malignancy.
Pain in the ear, either from a local cause within the ear (primary) or referred from a distant site such as the jaw, throat, or cervical spine (secondary).
Restricted mouth opening due to spasm or fibrosis of the masticatory muscles, associated with tetanus, temporomandibular disorders, and post-radiation fibrosis.
Diminished ability to experience pleasure from activities previously found enjoyable, a core symptom of major depressive disorder and schizophrenia.
A dull, steady, low-intensity pain that is poorly localized and tends to persist over time.
A vague, low-intensity pain that is diffuse and hard to pinpoint, felt more as pressure than a distinct point.
A sudden, intense, well-localized pain with a distinct cutting or pointed quality.
A sudden, penetrating pain that feels as though a pointed object is being thrust into the body.
A pain that travels rapidly along a path, often down a limb, in brief bursts.
Pain that spreads from its point of origin outward to nearby or more distant areas of the body.
A rhythmic, pulsing pain that rises and falls in time with the heartbeat.
Pain or discomfort felt when an area of the body is touched or pressed.
A mild, aching tenderness in muscle or tissue, typically felt with movement or pressure.
A persistent, dull, aching pain that feels as though something is slowly wearing at the area.
A severe pain, most often in the head, that feels as though the area is being cleaved apart.
A localized, sharp pain that feels as though tissue is being squeezed between two points.
An intense, heavy pain that feels as though the area is being compressed under great weight.
A sudden, severe pain that feels as though tissue is being ripped apart.
A pain that feels as though the affected area is being tightly gripped or compressed.
An intermittent, gripping pain caused by involuntary muscle contraction.
A sensation of weight or pressure in a body region, often felt alongside pain or fatigue.
A pain that comes in waves, building to a peak and easing off, caused by contraction of a hollow organ.
A deep, penetrating pain that feels as though it is drilling steadily into the body.
A pain felt as steady pushing or tightness, as though the area is being pressed.
A partial or complete loss of sensation in an area of the body.
A hot, stinging pain resembling the sensation of being burned, often arising from nerve irritation.
A sharp, prickling surface pain resembling an insect sting.
A brief, jolting pain that feels like a sudden electric shock, typically running along a nerve.
A sensation of insects crawling on or under the skin that occurs without any physical cause.
A painful sensation of intense cold in a body region, often linked to nerve dysfunction or cold exposure.
Pain of low intensity that is easily tolerated and does not interfere much with daily activity.
Pain of medium intensity that is noticeable and limits some activities but remains bearable.
Pain of high intensity that is difficult to tolerate and markedly interferes with daily activity.
Pain of extreme, barely tolerable intensity that overwhelms the person's ability to function.
Pain so severe that it causes intense anguish and torment.
Pain so intense that the person feels unable to endure or tolerate it.
Pain intense or deep enough to provoke nausea or a feeling of faintness.
Pain so sudden and intense that it momentarily overwhelms the senses.
Persistent pain that drains the person's energy and leaves them worn out.
A mild but persistent pain that continually bothers the person without being severe.
Pain that causes ongoing distress and unhappiness, wearing the person down over time.
Pain that is continuously present without letting up.
Pain that comes and goes, with pain-free periods between episodes.
Pain that lasts only a short moment before fading.
Pain that moves from one part of the body to another over time.
Pain felt in a part of the body away from its actual source.
Pain confined to a single, well-defined area of the body.
Pain that returns in repeated episodes over time.
Pain that gradually worsens in intensity over time.
The organ system of bones, muscles, joints, and connective tissues that supports the body, protects organs, and enables movement.
The organ system of the heart and blood vessels that circulates blood to deliver oxygen and nutrients and carry away waste.
The system of blood, bone marrow, and related organs that produces blood cells and transports oxygen, nutrients, and immune cells.
The network of cells, tissues, and organs that defends the body against infection, disease, and foreign substances.
The network of vessels, nodes, and organs that drains excess fluid from tissues, filters it, and supports immune defense.
The organ system of the airways and lungs that brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide.
The organ system that breaks down food, absorbs nutrients, and eliminates waste, extending from the mouth to the anus.
The organ system of the kidneys, ureters, bladder, and urethra that filters blood and removes waste as urine.
The organ system responsible for producing offspring, comprising the gonads and associated reproductive structures.
The system of glands that produce hormones to regulate metabolism, growth, mood, and other body functions.
The organ system of the brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls and coordinates body functions through electrical and chemical signals.
A person's emotional, psychological, and social well-being, affecting how they think, feel, cope with stress, and relate to others.
The organ system of the skin, hair, nails, and associated glands that covers and protects the body.
The parts of the nervous system that detect and process stimuli such as sight, sound, smell, taste, touch, and balance.
The branch of science concerned with the bodily structure of humans and the relationships between body parts.
Medical specialty that manages patients with life-threatening illness or injury, usually in an intensive care unit.
Medical specialty providing comprehensive, continuing care for individuals and families across all ages and conditions.
Broad field of medicine concerned with the diagnosis and nonsurgical treatment of a wide range of common conditions.
Field of medicine and biology concerned with heredity and the variation of inherited traits and disorders.
A nerve that carries signals away from the central nervous system to muscles or glands.
A nerve that carries sensory signals from the body toward the central nervous system.
A congenital defect in which brain tissue and its coverings protrude through an opening in the skull.
A serious birth defect in which a baby is born missing major portions of the brain and skull.
A depression at the front base of the skull, along the cribriform plate, where the olfactory bulbs rest.
The web-like middle membrane of the three layers covering the brain and spinal cord.
The tough, fibrous outermost membrane covering the brain and spinal cord.
The delicate innermost membrane that closely adheres to the surface of the brain and spinal cord.
A benign meningeal tumor arising at the junction of the petrous and clival bones at the skull base.
Damage to the brain caused by an external force such as a blow or jolt to the head.
Sudden, usually temporary weakness or paralysis of one side of the face caused by facial nerve dysfunction.
The branch of the vestibulocochlear nerve that carries balance information from the inner ear to the brain.
A nerve outside the brain and spinal cord that links the central nervous system to the rest of the body.
A test that measures how quickly and strongly electrical signals travel along a nerve.
A rapid electrical signal that travels along a nerve or muscle cell to transmit information.
A progressive neurodegenerative disease that destroys the motor neurons controlling voluntary muscle movement.
An autoimmune disorder in which the immune system attacks the peripheral nerves, causing rapid-onset muscle weakness.
A cause of positional vertigo in which calcium debris adheres to the cupula of a semicircular canal.
A cause of positional vertigo in which calcium debris floats freely within a semicircular canal.
A substance taken to stimulate sexual desire or enhance sexual performance.
A condition in which a person is born with sexual anatomy that does not fit typical male or female categories, formerly called hermaphroditism.
A persistent, often painful erection that continues long after sexual stimulation has ended.
A condition in which tissue resembling the uterine lining grows outside the uterus, causing pain and sometimes infertility.
Downward displacement of the uterus into or through the vagina due to weakened pelvic support.
A surgical incision of the perineum made during childbirth to widen the vaginal opening.
The region of the body between the anus and the external genitalia.
A procedure that widens the cervix and scrapes the uterine lining for diagnosis or to empty the uterus.
A prolapse in which the bladder bulges into the front wall of the vagina.
The accumulation of blood within a fallopian tube.
A local anesthetic injection that numbs the perineal region during childbirth.
A condition in which the placenta partly or completely covers the cervix, risking bleeding in pregnancy and delivery.
A serious pregnancy complication marked by seizures, developing from severe pre-eclampsia.
A pregnancy in which the fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, most often in a fallopian tube.
Inflammation of the muscular wall of the uterus.
A surgical procedure that fixes a prolapsed uterus in place through an abdominal approach.
Menstrual bleeding that is abnormal in amount, timing, or duration, the modern umbrella term that replaced older labels such as menorrhagia and metrorrhagia.
A common vaginal condition caused by an imbalance of the normal vaginal bacteria.
The deliberate prevention of pregnancy using drugs, devices, or other methods.
A physical examination of the female pelvic organs, including the vagina, cervix, uterus, and ovaries.
A hormonal disorder causing enlarged ovaries with small cysts, irregular periods, and excess androgen effects.
Examination of the cervix and vagina using a magnifying instrument called a colposcope.
A permanent method of female sterilization in which the fallopian tubes are cut or blocked.
Surgical removal of uterine fibroids while preserving the uterus.
A medical professional trained and licensed to diagnose and treat illness and injury.
A licensed clinician who examines, diagnoses, and treats patients under the supervision of a physician.
An advanced-practice nurse who can diagnose conditions, order tests, and prescribe treatment, often independently.
The senior physician with primary responsibility for a patient's care, often supervising residents and students.
A physician in postgraduate training who cares for patients under supervision after medical school.
A first-year resident physician in the earliest stage of postgraduate clinical training.
A physician receiving advanced subspecialty training after completing residency.
A physician who specializes in the care of patients while they are admitted to the hospital.
The main clinician a patient sees for routine care, prevention, and coordination of overall health.
A physician trained to treat disease and injury by operative procedures.
A trained professional who provides care during pregnancy, labor, and childbirth.
A licensed nurse who provides and coordinates patient care across clinical settings.
A nurse who provides basic bedside care under the direction of a registered nurse or physician.
A trained aide who helps patients with daily activities and basic care under nursing supervision.
A registered nurse who supervises and coordinates the nursing staff and patient flow on a unit during a shift.
An advanced-practice nurse who administers anesthesia and monitors patients during procedures.
A nurse who assesses incoming patients and prioritizes their care based on the urgency of their condition.
A technician trained to draw blood from patients for laboratory testing, transfusion, or donation.
A nurse who assists the surgical team within the sterile field during an operation.
A therapist who helps patients restore movement, strength, and function through exercise and physical techniques.
A therapist who helps patients regain the skills needed for daily activities and work after illness or injury.
A specialist who evaluates and treats disorders of speech, language, and swallowing.
A specialist who evaluates and treats patients with breathing disorders, often managing ventilators and oxygen therapy.
A specialist who evaluates, diagnoses, and treats hearing and balance disorders.
A specialist in nutrition who plans and advises on diets to promote health and manage disease.
A professional who helps patients and families cope with the social, emotional, and practical effects of illness.
A professional who coordinates a patient's care, services, and resources across providers and settings.
A technician trained to perform imaging examinations such as X-rays for diagnosis.
A staff member who performs routine clinical and administrative tasks to support physicians in outpatient settings.
A technician trained to perform ultrasound imaging of the body for diagnosis.
An emergency medical professional trained to provide advanced life support and care outside the hospital.
An emergency responder trained to provide basic life support and care at the scene and during transport.
A technician who performs laboratory tests on blood, tissue, and other samples to aid diagnosis.
An eye-care professional who examines vision, prescribes corrective lenses, and detects eye disorders.
A specialist who diagnoses and treats disorders of the foot, ankle, and lower leg.
A professional who studies mental processes and behavior and provides assessment and therapy for psychological conditions.
A practitioner who treats musculoskeletal problems, chiefly of the spine, mainly through manual adjustment.
A trained companion who provides continuous physical and emotional support to a person during pregnancy and childbirth.
A trained professional who provides guidance and talk therapy to help people manage emotional, mental, or life difficulties.
A traditional operation performed through a large incision that fully exposes the surgical site, unlike minimally invasive techniques.
Surgery performed on the heart with the chest opened, usually while a heart-lung machine maintains circulation.
The removal of the radial artery from the forearm for use as a graft in coronary bypass surgery.
The heart valve between the right atrium and right ventricle that prevents backflow of blood.
A rapid heart rhythm arising in the ventricles that can impair blood flow and become life-threatening.
A dangerous drop in blood pressure and tissue perfusion caused by severe loss of blood or fluid.
Any disease of the blood vessels.
Tissue death caused by an obstruction of the blood supply to the affected area.
A life-support technique that oxygenates the blood outside the body when the heart or lungs cannot function adequately.
Slow or shallow breathing that reduces oxygen intake and carbon dioxide removal, often from drugs or illness.
A disorder in which breathing repeatedly stops and starts during sleep.
Mucus coughed up from the lower airways, often examined to help diagnose respiratory disease.
The total amount of air a person can forcibly exhale after taking the deepest possible breath.
Shortness of breath that occurs without an identifiable physical cause in the lungs or heart.
Abnormally rapid or deep breathing that lowers the level of carbon dioxide in the blood.
A common virus that infects the nose and throat and is the leading cause of the common cold.
An imaging test that uses sound waves to measure blood flow through vessels.
A buildup of fluid in the lungs that impairs breathing, often caused by heart failure.
Enlargement and failure of the right side of the heart caused by chronic lung disease.
A condition in which the flaps of the mitral valve do not close smoothly, bulging upward into the left atrium during contraction.
The abnormal narrowing of a bodily passage, canal, or opening.
The backward flowing of blood through a damaged heart valve or of undigested food from the stomach.
A common, benign capillary vascular malformation appearing as a flat pink patch on a newborn's skin.
A vasodilator medication used to prevent or treat chest pain caused by reduced blood flow to the heart.
A surgical procedure that diverts blood around narrowed or clogged parts of the major arteries to improve blood flow to the heart.
A common cardiac arrhythmia characterized by premature heartbeats originating in the atria.
A benign tumor made up of newly formed blood vessels, clustering on the skin or internal organs.
A group of conditions characterized by the inflammation of and damage to blood vessels.
A state of abnormally low extracellular fluid volume in the body, typically due to blood or fluid loss.
An occupational lung disease caused by the chronic inhalation of mineral or metallic dust particles.
A surgical physician specialized in diagnosing and treating disorders of the colon, rectum, and anus.
A diagnostic method used to detect fecal occult blood that is not visible to the naked eye.
A surgical procedure connecting a bile duct directly to a portion of the small intestine.
The crushing of gallstones within the gallbladder or bile ducts using shock waves.
A surgical incision made into the gallbladder or bile duct to remove gallstones.
An excess of air or gas inside the stomach, often leading to bloating and distension.
The duct formed by the junction of the hepatic and cystic ducts that carries bile into the duodenum.

Any degenerative disease of the kidney tubules without significant inflammation.
A condition in which a person wakes up multiple times during the night to urinate.
A congenital birth defect in males where the opening of the urethra is on the underside of the penis rather than at the tip.
A fluid-filled sac surrounding a testicle, resulting in swelling of the scrotum.
A condition in which one or both of the testes fail to descend from the abdomen into the scrotum.
A surgical procedure used to treat urinary problems due to an enlarged prostate by removing tissue through the urethra.
A surgical procedure performed to ligate and remove enlarged veins within the scrotum.
A surgical procedure for removing kidney stones through an incision made directly into the kidney.
A medical procedure that breaks up kidney stones inside the body using shock waves or lasers.
A blockage at the point where the renal pelvis connects to the ureter, impeding urine flow from the kidney.
A group of autoimmune diseases causing hardening and tightening of the skin and connective tissues, sometimes affecting internal organs.
A topical product that reduces underarm sweating by temporarily blocking sweat glands.
A microscopic diagnostic test using potassium hydroxide solution to dissolve skin cells and reveal fungal elements in samples from skin, hair, or nails.
A sudden worsening of symptoms in a chronic medical condition.
Any disease caused by a fungal infection.
A painful open sore on the mucous membrane inside the mouth.

A genus of gram-positive bacteria that can cause a wide range of infections including strep throat and pneumonia.
The bacterium Streptococcus pneumoniae, a leading cause of community-acquired pneumonia, meningitis, and other invasive infections.
A fungal infection of the mouth caused by overgrowth of Candida species, often presenting as white patches.
A highly contagious bacterial respiratory infection characterized by severe coughing fits.
A serious bacterial infection causing a thick gray coating in the throat and potentially leading to heart and nerve damage.
A serious bacterial disease causing painful muscle spasms and stiffness, often from wound contamination.
A combination vaccine protecting against tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis.
A painful rash caused by reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus, typically in a dermatomal distribution.
A tick-borne bacterial infection that can cause rash, fever, and later joint, heart, or nervous system problems.

The way an infant attaches to the breast during breastfeeding to effectively extract milk.
A group of muscles and tendons that stabilize the shoulder joint and enable arm rotation and lifting.
Inflammation of a bursa, a fluid-filled sac that cushions joints and reduces friction between tissues.
An autoimmune neuromuscular disorder causing fluctuating muscle weakness and fatigue.
A group of genetic diseases causing progressive weakness and degeneration of skeletal muscles.
Pain in one or more joints without evident inflammation.
A diagnostic test measuring electrical activity in muscles to assess neuromuscular function.

Surgical suturing of a torn or severed tendon.
A specialized cell that produces and maintains the cartilage matrix in joints.

An inflammatory disease causing muscle weakness, particularly in the proximal limbs.
